metadata.description: Complete plan to take a raw product idea from concept through launched, monitored software product. Designed for AI agent execution with human-in-the-loop via needs_clarification mechanism.
metadata.clarification_mechanism: Any agent can trigger needs_clarification status. The assistant agent formulates the question, sends via Telegram, interprets the human reply, and resumes the task with clarification context.
metadata.artifact_convention: Each step reads input_artifacts from prior steps and produces output_artifacts. The orchestrator must pass artifact contents as context to the executing agent.
metadata.template_convention: Steps with type=template are reusable sequences. They are instantiated by referencing the template_id and providing template_params. The feature_backlog lists items that each instantiate the feature_implementation_template.

phases.name: Idea Capture & Clarification
phases.goal: Turn a vague idea into a crisp, validated problem statement with clear constraints.

phases.name: Market & Competitive Research
phases.goal: Understand the market landscape, competitors, user needs, and regulatory environment.

phases.name: Product Strategy & Definition
phases.goal: Define what we are building, for whom, why it wins, and what the MVP scope is.

phases.name: Requirements Engineering
phases.goal: Convert product requirements into precise, testable technical requirements.

phases.name: Architecture & Technical Design
phases.goal: Design the complete technical foundation: stack, schema, APIs, infrastructure patterns.

phases.name: UX/UI Design Specification
phases.goal: Fully specify every screen, component, interaction, and responsive behavior.
phases.note: Can run in parallel with phase_4. Does not depend on tech stack decisions.

phases.name: Project Planning & Sprint Setup
phases.goal: Break all work into estimated, ordered, dependency-mapped sprints.

phases.name: Development Environment Setup
phases.goal: Set up repositories, tooling, CI/CD, infrastructure, and design system implementation.

phases.name: Core Implementation
phases.goal: Build every MVP feature through repeatable implementation cycles.

phases.name: Comprehensive Testing
phases.goal: Ensure reliability, performance, accessibility, and cross-platform correctness.

phases.name: Security Hardening
phases.goal: Audit and fix all security vulnerabilities before public exposure.

phases.name: Documentation
phases.goal: Document everything for users, developers, and operators.

phases.name: Legal & Compliance
phases.goal: Ensure legal coverage: ToS, privacy policy, GDPR, license compliance.

phases.name: Pre-Launch Preparation
phases.goal: Production infrastructure, monitoring, analytics, and final QA.

phases.name: Launch
phases.goal: Go live safely and announce to the world.

phases.name: Post-Launch Operations & Iteration
phases.goal: Monitor, stabilize, collect feedback, iterate, and grow.

steps.name: raw_idea_intake
steps.instruction: Take the raw idea input provided by the human. Document it exactly as stated, preserving all details, aspirations, mentioned features, and constraints. Do not interpret, filter, or add anything. Structure the output as a document with sections: Original Statement, Mentioned Features, Mentioned Constraints, Mentioned Audience, Mentioned Goals. If any section has no content from the raw idea, mark it as 'Not specified'.
steps.done_when: A structured raw_idea_document exists that faithfully represents the original idea with zero interpretation added.

steps.name: problem_statement_extraction
steps.instruction: From the raw_idea_document, extract and articulate the core problem being solved. Output a structured problem statement with these fields: problem_description (1-3 sentences describing the pain), affected_audience (who has this problem), current_alternatives (how they cope today, if inferable), severity (how painful this is, if inferable), frequency (how often they encounter it, if inferable). Mark any field as 'Needs validation' if it cannot be confidently inferred from the raw idea.
steps.done_when: A structured problem_statement exists with all fields populated or explicitly marked as needing validation.

steps.name: assumption_identification
steps.instruction: Identify every assumption embedded in the raw idea. For each assumption, output: assumption_id (A-001, A-002...), statement (the assumption in clear language), category (one of: market, user, technical, business, legal, competitive), risk_if_wrong (what happens if this assumption is false), validation_method (how we could validate this). Common categories to check: 'Users exist who want this', 'Users will pay for this', 'This is technically feasible', 'No legal blockers exist', 'We can differentiate from competitors', 'The market is large enough'.
steps.done_when: A numbered assumption_list exists with at least 5 assumptions identified, each with all fields completed.

steps.name: scope_ambiguity_detection
steps.instruction: Review all input artifacts. Identify every ambiguity, undefined term, or scope question that must be resolved before proceeding. Generate a numbered list of open questions organized by category: Audience (who exactly?), Scope (what's in/out?), Business Model (how does this make money?), Constraints (budget, timeline, tech, team?), Platform (web, mobile, desktop?), Differentiation (why us over competitors?), Priority (what matters most: speed, quality, features, cost?). Each question should be specific enough that a yes/no or short answer resolves it.
steps.done_when: An open_questions_list exists with all identified ambiguities as clear, answerable questions.

steps.name: human_clarification_request
steps.instruction: Review the open_questions_list and assumption_list. Prioritize the questions by impact (which answers most change the direction of the project). Format the top questions (max 10) into a clear, numbered message for the human. For each question, provide brief context on why it matters and suggest 2-3 possible answers if applicable. Trigger needs_clarification to send this to the human. When the response arrives, parse the answers and map each back to the original question ID. If answers are ambiguous or incomplete, trigger needs_clarification again for follow-up.
steps.done_when: clarification_answers document exists mapping each asked question to a clear answer from the human.

steps.name: idea_brief_compilation
steps.instruction: Compile all inputs into a clean Idea Brief document with these sections: 1) Vision (one paragraph describing the aspiration), 2) Problem Statement (from problem_statement, updated with clarifications), 3) Target Audience (specific, from clarifications), 4) Proposed Solution (high-level, what the product does), 5) Key Constraints (budget, timeline, tech, from clarifications), 6) Initial Scope Boundaries (what's in for v1, what's explicitly out), 7) Assumptions (updated list, marking which were validated by clarifications), 8) Open Risks (remaining unknowns). Write in clear, professional prose. This document is the foundation for all subsequent work.
steps.done_when: A complete idea_brief exists with all 8 sections populated, internally consistent, and reflecting human clarifications.

steps.name: idea_brief_review
steps.instruction: Review the idea_brief against the original raw_idea_document and clarification_answers. Check for: 1) Internal contradictions between sections, 2) Anything from clarification_answers not reflected, 3) Anything from raw idea lost or misrepresented, 4) Vague language that should be specific, 5) Missing information that should be present. Output a review result with: status (approved/needs_revision), issues (list of specific problems with section references), suggestions (improvements). If status is approved, issues list should be empty.
steps.done_when: idea_brief_review_result exists with clear status and actionable issues list (may be empty if approved).

steps.name: idea_brief_finalization
steps.instruction: If idea_brief_review_result status is 'approved', copy idea_brief to idea_brief_final unchanged. If status is 'needs_revision', address every issue listed in the review: fix contradictions, add missing information, clarify vague language. Do not change content that was not flagged. Output the final versioned document as idea_brief_final with a version number and date.
steps.done_when: idea_brief_final exists as a clean, reviewed, internally consistent document.

steps.name: market_existence_research
steps.instruction: Using the idea_brief_final, research whether a market exists for this problem. Search for: market size estimates (TAM, SAM, SOM if available), market growth rate, industry reports or analyst coverage, investment activity in this space (recent funding rounds), Google Trends data for related keywords, job postings in this space (indicates industry activity). Use Perplexica for web search. Check industry-specific sources. Output structured findings: market_size (with source), growth_trend (with source), investment_signals (list of recent fundings), search_interest_trend (direction), industry_reports (list with links), confidence_level (high/medium/low with justification).
steps.done_when: market_existence_data exists with sourced data points covering market size, growth, and investment activity.

steps.name: target_audience_research
steps.instruction: Research the target audience defined in idea_brief_final. Find: demographics (age, location, occupation, income level), psychographics (values, attitudes, lifestyle), online behavior (which platforms, subreddits, forums, communities they use), pain points (what they complain about related to this problem), current tools/solutions they use, willingness to pay signals (do they pay for similar tools?), technology adoption level. Search Reddit, Twitter/X, forums, Quora, Stack Overflow, Product Hunt discussions, industry surveys. Output structured findings per category with source links.
steps.done_when: audience_research_data exists with demographic, psychographic, behavioral, and pain point data with sources.

steps.name: direct_competitor_identification
steps.instruction: Identify all direct competitors: products solving the same problem for the same audience. For each competitor, collect: name, website_url, one_line_description, founding_year, funding_total (if known), team_size_estimate, platforms (web/ios/android/desktop), primary_market (geography), status (active/acquired/dead). Search: Perplexica web search, Product Hunt, G2, Capterra, AlternativeTo, Crunchbase, App Store, Play Store. Find at least 5 competitors if they exist. If fewer than 3 exist, note this as a market signal (either blue ocean or no market).
steps.done_when: direct_competitors_list exists as a structured list with all fields populated per competitor.

steps.name: indirect_competitor_identification
steps.instruction: Identify indirect competitors: products that solve the same problem differently, or solve an adjacent problem for the same audience. Examples: spreadsheets competing with project management tools, email competing with Slack. For each, collect: name, website_url, how_they_relate (same problem different approach, or adjacent problem same audience), threat_level (high/medium/low — how likely users choose this instead). Also identify potential future competitors: large companies that could enter this space easily.
steps.done_when: indirect_competitors_list exists with categorized indirect competitors and future threat assessment.

steps.name: competitor_feature_extraction
steps.instruction: For each direct competitor in direct_competitors_list, catalog every feature visible from their: marketing website (features page, pricing page), documentation, free tier exploration, App Store/Play Store listing, Product Hunt page, demo videos. For each feature, record: feature_name, feature_description, which_competitor, tier_available (free/paid/enterprise), and category (core, secondary, integration, admin). Be exhaustive — capture everything including small features like 'dark mode' or 'CSV export'.
steps.done_when: competitor_features_raw exists with a comprehensive feature list per competitor.

steps.name: competitor_feature_matrix
steps.instruction: From competitor_features_raw, build a feature comparison matrix. Rows: every unique feature (deduplicated and normalized across competitors). Columns: each competitor. Cell values: 'full' (feature fully supported), 'partial' (limited version), 'none' (not available), 'unknown' (couldn't determine). Group rows by feature category. Add a 'feature_frequency' column showing how many competitors have each feature (indicates table stakes). Sort within each category by frequency descending. Output as structured table/JSON.
steps.done_when: competitor_feature_matrix exists as a structured comparison table with all competitors and all features cross-referenced.

steps.name: competitor_pricing_analysis
steps.instruction: For each direct competitor, document their pricing model completely: pricing_model_type (free, freemium, free_trial, subscription, one_time, usage_based, open_source), tiers (list each tier with: name, monthly_price, annual_price, key_limits), free_tier_scope (what's included free), upgrade_triggers (what forces users to upgrade), enterprise_offering (custom pricing, self-hosted option?), pricing_page_url. Then analyze: what is the market's standard pricing range, what's the most common model, where are pricing gaps (underserved segments), what features gate free→paid transition.
steps.done_when: competitor_pricing_analysis exists with per-competitor pricing details and cross-market pricing insights.

steps.name: competitor_review_mining
steps.instruction: For each major competitor (top 5), collect user reviews and sentiment from: App Store, Play Store, G2, Capterra, TrustRadius, Product Hunt comments, Reddit mentions (search 'competitor_name' in relevant subreddits), Twitter/X mentions, Hacker News discussions. For each source, collect: source_name, review_count_sampled, average_rating (if available), positive_themes (recurring praise topics), negative_themes (recurring complaint topics), notable_quotes (3-5 representative quotes per competitor covering both positive and negative). Focus on recent reviews (last 12 months).
steps.done_when: competitor_reviews_raw exists with review themes and notable quotes per competitor from multiple sources.

steps.name: competitor_sentiment_analysis
steps.instruction: From competitor_reviews_raw, synthesize sentiment across all competitors. Output: 1) Per competitor: top_5_praised_aspects (with frequency estimate), top_5_pain_points (with frequency estimate), overall_sentiment (positive/mixed/negative), net_promoter_signal (would users recommend? based on review tone). 2) Cross-competitor: universal_praises (things ALL competitors do well — table stakes), universal_complaints (things ALL competitors fail at — opportunities for us), differentiation_signals (things only one competitor does well — potential unique features).
steps.done_when: competitor_sentiment_analysis exists with per-competitor and cross-competitor sentiment synthesis.

steps.name: competitor_screenshot_collection
steps.instruction: For each major competitor (top 5), collect screenshots or visual descriptions of key screens: landing_page, signup_flow, onboarding, main_dashboard, core_feature_screen, settings_page, pricing_page, mobile_view (if available). Source from: competitor website, App Store screenshots, Product Hunt gallery, demo videos (key frames), review sites with screenshots. Store each with metadata: competitor_name, screen_name, source_url, description of what's shown.
steps.done_when: competitor_screenshots collection exists with key screens captured or described per competitor.

steps.name: competitor_ux_evaluation
steps.instruction: Review competitor_screenshots for each competitor. Evaluate: design_quality (1-10 with justification), visual_consistency (consistent design system?), information_density (cluttered vs clean), navigation_clarity (is it obvious how to get around?), onboarding_quality (is first experience guided?), accessibility_signals (visible contrast issues, small text, missing labels?), mobile_quality (responsive or native? quality?), unique_ux_patterns (anything innovative worth noting), ux_weaknesses (confusing flows, poor hierarchy, bad patterns). Output per competitor with overall_ux_rank ordering competitors from best to worst UX.
steps.done_when: competitor_ux_evaluation exists with per-competitor UX assessment and comparative ranking.

steps.name: competitor_swot_analysis
steps.instruction: For each major competitor (top 3-5), produce a SWOT analysis. Strengths: what they do well (features, UX, brand, pricing, market position). Weaknesses: where they fail (user complaints, feature gaps, poor UX, high pricing). Opportunities: what they could exploit but haven't (market gaps, tech trends, underserved segments). Threats: what could hurt them (new entrants like us, technology shifts, regulation, market changes). Each SWOT item should be a specific, evidence-based statement referencing data from input artifacts, not generic observations.
steps.done_when: competitor_swot_analyses exists with evidence-based SWOT for each major competitor.

steps.name: market_gap_analysis
steps.instruction: Cross-reference: features that users request but no competitor provides (from sentiment), features where all competitors are 'partial' (from matrix), SWOT weaknesses common across competitors, pricing gaps (underserved segments). Output a ranked list of market opportunities: opportunity_id, description, evidence (which data points support this), estimated_impact (high/medium/low), difficulty_to_address (high/medium/low), our_ability_to_exploit (high/medium/low based on idea_brief constraints). Rank by impact × feasibility. This is the core strategic input for product positioning.
steps.done_when: market_gap_analysis exists with a ranked, evidence-based list of market opportunities.

steps.name: technology_trend_research
steps.instruction: Research technology trends relevant to the product domain. Find: new APIs or services that enable functionality that was previously hard, AI/ML capabilities relevant to the product, emerging platforms or distribution channels, open-source tools that reduce build cost, developer productivity improvements relevant to our stack. Sources: Hacker News trends, GitHub trending, tech blogs, API directories, AI model releases. For each trend: name, description, relevance_to_our_product, maturity_level (experimental/emerging/mature), adoption_risk.
steps.done_when: technology_trends document exists with relevant tech trends and their applicability assessed.

steps.name: regulatory_research
steps.instruction: Identify legal, regulatory, and compliance requirements for this product. Check: data privacy (GDPR if EU users, CCPA if California, other regional), industry-specific regulation (HIPAA for health, PCI-DSS for payments, COPPA for children, FINRA for finance), platform policies (App Store guidelines, Play Store policies), accessibility law (ADA, Section 508, EN 301 549), intellectual property concerns, open-source license obligations. For each: requirement_name, applies_if (condition), summary_of_obligations, compliance_effort (low/medium/high), consequence_of_non_compliance.
steps.done_when: regulatory_requirements exists with all applicable regulations identified and obligations summarized.

steps.name: research_synthesis
steps.instruction: Compress all research findings into a structured Research Summary with sections: 1) Market Viability (2-3 sentences: does the market exist and is it growing?), 2) Target Audience Profile (2-3 sentences: who are they and what do they need?), 3) Competitive Landscape (3-5 sentences: how crowded, who leads, common weaknesses), 4) Top 3 Market Opportunities (from gap analysis, one sentence each), 5) Technology Enablers (key tech that gives us an edge), 6) Key Risks (top 3 regulatory, market, or competitive risks), 7) Go/No-Go Signal (one sentence: positive, negative, or conditional). Keep the entire summary under 500 words.
steps.done_when: research_summary exists as a concise executive summary under 500 words.

steps.name: market_research_report
steps.instruction: Compile all research into a comprehensive Market Research Report. Structure: Executive Summary (from research_summary), Market Overview, Target Audience Analysis, Competitive Landscape (with feature matrix, pricing comparison, UX comparison, SWOT analyses), Market Gap Analysis, Technology Landscape, Regulatory Environment, Recommendations. Each section should reference source data. Include the feature matrix and pricing comparison as structured tables. This is the definitive research document.
steps.done_when: market_research_report exists as a comprehensive, well-structured document incorporating all research artifacts.

steps.name: research_quality_review
steps.instruction: Review market_research_report for: 1) Claims without evidence or sources, 2) Logical gaps in reasoning, 3) Missing competitor or market data that should be obvious, 4) Bias (overly optimistic or pessimistic conclusions), 5) Internal contradictions, 6) Completeness of the feature matrix and pricing analysis, 7) Quality of the gap analysis (are opportunities real or wishful thinking?). Output: status (approved/needs_revision), issues list with specific references.
steps.done_when: research_review_result exists with clear quality assessment.

steps.name: go_no_go_assessment
steps.instruction: Evaluate all research to produce a Go/No-Go recommendation. Score these factors 1-10: market_attractiveness (size, growth, willingness to pay), competitive_feasibility (can we win against existing players), technical_feasibility (can we build this with available tech), differentiation_potential (can we be meaningfully different), regulatory_risk (inverse: high score = low risk). Compute weighted_score. If score > 6: recommend Go with key conditions. If score 4-6: recommend Conditional — present the conditions and trade-offs, trigger needs_clarification to ask human to decide. If score < 4: recommend No-Go with clear reasoning. Output: scores, weighted_score, recommendation, conditions, key_risks.
steps.done_when: go_no_go_decision exists with scored assessment and clear recommendation. If conditional, human has been consulted and decision is recorded.

steps.name: product_vision_statement
steps.instruction: Craft a product vision statement: 1-2 sentences describing the aspirational future state this product creates. It should be inspiring, specific to this product (not generic), and testable in the long run. Format: 'For [target audience] who [need], [product name] is a [category] that [key benefit]. Unlike [competitors], we [key differentiator].' Also write a shorter tagline version (under 10 words).
steps.done_when: product_vision exists with both full vision statement and tagline.

steps.name: product_mission_statement
steps.instruction: Craft a product mission statement: what the product does today, for whom, and how. Unlike the vision (aspirational future), the mission is grounded in current reality. Format: 'We help [specific audience] [achieve specific outcome] by [how the product does it].' Keep it under 30 words, concrete, and jargon-free.
steps.done_when: product_mission exists as a clear, concrete statement under 30 words.

steps.name: value_proposition_canvas
steps.instruction: Create a Value Proposition Canvas with two sides. Customer Profile: customer_jobs (functional, social, emotional tasks they try to accomplish), customer_pains (frustrations, risks, obstacles), customer_gains (desired outcomes, benefits they seek). Value Map: products_and_services (what we offer), pain_relievers (how we address each pain), gain_creators (how we enable each gain). Draw explicit connections: each pain_reliever must reference a specific customer_pain. Each gain_creator must reference a specific customer_gain. Identify the top 3 most important pain-gain fits.
steps.done_when: value_proposition_canvas exists with both sides populated and explicit pain-gain connections identified.

steps.name: unique_selling_proposition
steps.instruction: Define 1-3 USPs that differentiate this product from every competitor. Each USP must be: 1) Valuable (users care about this), 2) Unique (no competitor does this well), 3) Credible (we can actually deliver this), 4) Communicable (expressible in one sentence). Reference specific competitor weaknesses and market gaps. If multiple positioning strategies are viable (e.g., 'best UX' vs 'most features' vs 'cheapest'), present all options with trade-offs and trigger needs_clarification to ask human to choose direction.
steps.done_when: unique_selling_proposition exists with 1-3 validated USPs. If multiple strategies existed, human has chosen direction.

steps.name: user_persona_creation
steps.instruction: Create 2-4 user personas representing distinct segments of the target audience. Each persona: name (realistic), age, occupation, location, tech_savviness (1-10), income_level, goals (3 bullet points), frustrations (3 bullet points), current_tools (what they use today), behavioral_patterns (when/how they'd use our product), quote (one sentence capturing their core need), primary_pain (from value proposition canvas). One persona should be the 'primary' persona (most important to serve first). Personas must be distinct from each other, not variations of the same person.
steps.done_when: user_personas exists with 2-4 distinct, detailed personas with a primary persona identified.

steps.name: user_scenario_writing
steps.instruction: For each user persona, write 2-3 usage scenarios. Each scenario: scenario_name, persona, context (when and where they encounter the problem), trigger (what prompts them to use the product), workflow (step-by-step what they do in the product, 5-10 steps), outcome (what they achieve), emotional_state_before (frustrated, confused, etc.), emotional_state_after (relieved, satisfied, etc.). Scenarios should cover: first-time use, regular use, and an edge case or advanced use. Write in narrative form, third person.
steps.done_when: user_scenarios exists with 2-3 narrative scenarios per persona covering different usage contexts.

steps.name: feature_brainstorm
steps.instruction: Generate an exhaustive list of every possible feature. Sources of features: 1) Every feature in competitor_feature_matrix (table stakes), 2) Every pain reliever and gain creator from value_proposition_canvas, 3) Every step in user_scenarios where the product does something, 4) Technology-enabled features from technology_trends, 5) Features implied by the USP. No filtering at this stage — include everything from 'user can create an account' to 'AI-powered recommendation engine'. For each: feature_id (F-001...), feature_name, description (one sentence), source (which input artifact suggested this), category (auth, core, secondary, integration, admin, analytics, social).
steps.done_when: feature_brainstorm_list exists with an exhaustive, categorized feature list. Expect 50-200 features.

steps.name: feature_prioritization
steps.instruction: Categorize every feature using MoSCoW: Must-have (product doesn't work without it, or USP requires it), Should-have (significant value but workaround exists), Could-have (nice but not critical for launch), Won't-have (future consideration, explicitly deferred). For each feature, justify the categorization in one sentence. Must-haves should include: core auth, the minimum features to deliver the primary value proposition, and any features required by regulatory requirements. Count: aim for 15-25 Must-haves, 10-20 Should-haves.
steps.done_when: feature_prioritization exists with every feature categorized and justified.

steps.name: mvp_scope_definition
steps.instruction: Define the MVP scope: the smallest feature set that delivers the core value proposition and validates the primary USP. Include all Must-haves. Selectively include Should-haves that directly support the USP or are needed for a coherent user experience. The MVP must pass this test: 'A user from our primary persona can complete the core workflow end-to-end and receive the primary value.' Output: mvp_feature_list (subset of feature_prioritization), excluded_features_rationale (why each Should-have was included or excluded), estimated_scope_size (S/M/L), mvp_value_statement (one sentence: what a user can do with this MVP). If the scope feels too large (>30 features) or too small (<8 features), trigger needs_clarification to validate with human.
steps.done_when: mvp_scope exists with a clear feature list, rationale, and scope size estimate.

steps.name: use_case_writing
steps.instruction: For every feature in mvp_scope.mvp_feature_list, write a formal use case. Each use case: use_case_id (UC-001...), feature_id (reference), title, primary_actor (which persona), preconditions (system state before), trigger (what starts the flow), main_flow (numbered steps, 3-15 steps), alternate_flows (variations: different inputs, optional paths), exception_flows (error conditions: invalid input, permission denied, network failure, concurrent edit), postconditions (system state after success), business_rules (references to any business rules that apply). Write from the user's perspective, not the system's.
steps.done_when: use_cases exists with a formal use case for every MVP feature, including alternate and exception flows.

steps.name: user_story_writing
steps.instruction: Convert each use case into one or more user stories. Each user story: story_id (US-001...), use_case_id (reference), epic (feature category), title, story ('As a [persona name], I want [specific action], so that [specific benefit]'), acceptance_criteria (list of Given/When/Then statements covering: happy path, key alternate flows, key error cases), priority (from MoSCoW), estimated_complexity (XS/S/M/L/XL based on technical intuition). A complex use case may produce 2-5 user stories. Simple ones produce 1. Every acceptance criterion must be testable by an automated test.
steps.done_when: user_stories exists with testable user stories covering all MVP use cases, each with Given/When/Then acceptance criteria.

steps.name: user_journey_mapping
steps.instruction: Map the end-to-end user journey for the primary persona through these stages: awareness (how they discover us), consideration (what convinces them to try), signup (registration flow), onboarding (first-run experience), first_value (moment they get the core benefit), engagement (regular use pattern), mastery (advanced usage), expansion (upgrade, invite others), advocacy (refer, review). For each stage: user_actions, touchpoints (which screens/emails), user_emotions, potential_drop_off_reasons, metrics_to_track. Identify the 'aha moment' — the first time the user experiences the core value.
steps.done_when: user_journey_map exists with all stages, the 'aha moment' identified, and drop-off risks noted.

steps.name: success_metrics_definition
steps.instruction: Define measurable KPIs organized by the pirate metrics framework (AARRR): Acquisition (signup rate, traffic sources, cost per acquisition), Activation (onboarding completion rate, time to first value, aha moment rate), Retention (D1/D7/D30 retention, WAU/MAU ratio, churn rate), Revenue (MRR, ARPU, conversion rate free→paid, LTV), Referral (NPS score, invite rate, viral coefficient). For each KPI: name, formula, data_source (how to measure), target_value (initial goal), measurement_frequency (daily/weekly/monthly). Also define: north_star_metric (the single most important metric) with justification.
steps.done_when: success_metrics exists with AARRR metrics, targets, measurement methods, and a north star metric identified.

steps.name: monetization_strategy
steps.instruction: Define the business model. Based on competitor_pricing_analysis and our positioning, recommend: pricing_model (freemium, free_trial, subscription, usage_based, one_time — justify), tiers (for each tier: name, monthly_price, annual_price, included_features from mvp_scope, limits), free_tier_strategy (what's free and why — should drive activation), upgrade_triggers (what motivates free→paid), billing_implementation (monthly/annual/both, proration, trial_length), revenue_projections (back-of-napkin: if X users at Y% conversion at $Z, MRR = ...). If multiple viable models exist (e.g., freemium vs free trial), present both with trade-offs and trigger needs_clarification.
steps.done_when: monetization_strategy exists with pricing tiers, free/paid split, and revenue projections.

steps.name: prd_compilation
steps.instruction: Compile everything into a single Product Requirements Document (PRD). Sections: 1) Product Vision & Mission, 2) Target Audience & Personas, 3) User Scenarios, 4) Value Proposition & USP, 5) MVP Feature Scope (with feature list and rationale), 6) User Stories (all, with acceptance criteria), 7) User Journey Map, 8) Success Metrics & KPIs, 9) Monetization Strategy, 10) Out of Scope (explicitly deferred features), 11) Open Questions & Risks. Each section should reference its source artifacts. Add a version number and table of contents.
steps.done_when: prd exists as a comprehensive, structured document with all 11 sections populated.

steps.name: prd_review
steps.instruction: Review the PRD for: 1) Alignment with idea_brief_final (did we drift from the original vision?), 2) Internal consistency (do user stories match personas? do metrics match features?), 3) Completeness (every MVP feature has stories? every story has acceptance criteria?), 4) Feasibility (any features that seem technically impossible or extremely costly?), 5) Conflicts (user stories that contradict each other), 6) Testability (can every acceptance criterion be automated?), 7) Scope creep (did Must-haves inflate beyond reasonable?). Output: status (approved/needs_revision), issues list with severity and section references.
steps.done_when: prd_review_result exists with thorough assessment.

steps.name: prd_finalization
steps.instruction: If prd_review_result.status is 'approved', copy to prd_final with version bump. If 'needs_revision', address every issue: fix contradictions, fill gaps, adjust scope, improve testability of criteria. Preserve content not flagged. Output final versioned PRD.
steps.done_when: prd_final exists as a clean, reviewed, internally consistent PRD.

steps.name: functional_requirements_extraction
steps.instruction: Extract every functional requirement from prd_final. A functional requirement describes something the system must DO. For each: req_id (FR-001...), title, description, source_story_ids (which user stories drive this), priority (must/should/could), category (auth, data, ui, api, integration, notification, search, admin, billing). Examples: 'FR-001: System must allow users to register with email and password', 'FR-012: System must send email verification after registration'. Extract from: user stories main flows, alternate flows, exception flows, business rules. Every user story should map to at least one FR. Every FR should trace back to at least one user story.
steps.done_when: functional_requirements exists as a numbered list where every requirement traces to user stories and every user story traces to requirements.

steps.name: nfr_performance
steps.instruction: Define performance non-functional requirements. For each: req_id (NFR-PERF-001...), metric, target_value, measurement_method, rationale. Required metrics: api_response_time_p50 (target: under 200ms for reads, under 500ms for writes), api_response_time_p95 (target: under 500ms reads, under 1000ms writes), api_response_time_p99 (target: under 1000ms reads, under 2000ms writes), page_load_time (target: under 3s on 3G, under 1.5s on broadband), time_to_interactive (target: under 3.5s), largest_contentful_paint (target: under 2.5s), first_input_delay (target: under 100ms), cumulative_layout_shift (target: under 0.1), throughput (target: requests per second the system must handle at baseline), database_query_time (target: p95 under 100ms for common queries). Adjust targets based on product type and audience expectations from research.
steps.done_when: nfr_performance exists with measurable performance targets for all key metrics.

steps.name: nfr_scalability
steps.instruction: Define scalability non-functional requirements. For each: req_id (NFR-SCALE-001...), dimension, current_target, growth_target, scaling_approach. Dimensions: concurrent_users (how many simultaneous users at launch, at 6 months, at 12 months), data_volume (expected database size at launch, growth rate per month), file_storage_volume (expected media storage growth), api_request_volume (requests per minute at launch and projected growth), background_job_volume (jobs per hour), notification_volume (emails/notifications per day). Base projections on success_metrics targets and market size from research. Include a 10x headroom factor for burst scenarios.
steps.done_when: nfr_scalability exists with current and projected scale targets for all dimensions.

steps.name: nfr_availability
steps.instruction: Define availability non-functional requirements: req_id (NFR-AVAIL-001...), uptime_target (e.g., 99.9% = 8.7 hours downtime per year), planned_maintenance_window (when and how long acceptable), maximum_unplanned_downtime (per incident), recovery_time_objective_RTO (how fast must we recover), recovery_point_objective_RPO (how much data loss is acceptable), degraded_mode_behavior (what happens when a dependency is down: cache serves stale data? feature disabled? maintenance page?). For MVP, 99.5% uptime is typically acceptable. Define per service if architecture has multiple components.
steps.done_when: nfr_availability exists with uptime targets, RTO, RPO, and degraded mode behaviors defined.

steps.name: security_requirements
steps.instruction: Define security requirements. For each: req_id (NFR-SEC-001...), category, requirement, rationale. Categories and specific requirements to address: authentication (password policy, hashing algorithm, brute force protection, session management, token expiry, MFA requirement), authorization (access control model, principle of least privilege, resource isolation between users/tenants), data_protection (encryption at rest, encryption in transit, PII handling, data masking in logs, backup encryption), api_security (rate limiting, input validation, CORS policy, API key management), infrastructure (security headers, WAF, DDoS protection, secret management, dependency scanning), audit (audit log requirements: what events to log, retention period, tamper resistance), compliance (map each regulatory_requirement to specific technical controls needed).
steps.done_when: security_requirements exists with categorized security requirements traceable to regulatory obligations.

steps.name: data_requirements
steps.instruction: Define data requirements by analyzing what data the system must handle. For each data entity: entity_name, description, data_fields (list with types), sensitivity_level (public, internal, confidential, restricted), volume_estimate (records at launch, growth rate), retention_policy (how long to keep, legal basis), deletion_requirements (can user request deletion? cascade effects?), backup_requirements (frequency, retention). Also define: data_flows (which data moves between systems), data_transformations (any processing, aggregation, or derived data), data_access_patterns (read-heavy vs write-heavy, query patterns), data_archival_strategy (when does data move to cold storage).
steps.done_when: data_requirements exists with all data entities, their sensitivity, volume, retention, and access patterns defined.

steps.name: integration_requirements
steps.instruction: Identify all third-party integrations needed. For each: integration_id (INT-001...), service_name, purpose (what it does for us), api_type (REST, GraphQL, SDK, webhook), authentication_method (API key, OAuth, JWT), rate_limits (requests per minute/hour), pricing (free tier limits, cost at expected volume), data_exchanged (what we send, what we receive), latency_expectation (sync vs async), failure_mode (what happens if this service is down), alternatives (backup services). Common integrations to evaluate: payment (Stripe, Paddle), email (SendGrid, Postmark, SES), auth (Auth0, Clerk, built-in), storage (S3, Cloudflare R2), analytics (Mixpanel, PostHog, Amplitude), search (Algolia, Meilisearch, Typesense), monitoring (Sentry, Datadog).
steps.done_when: integration_requirements exists with every third-party service identified, evaluated, and failure modes documented.

steps.name: accessibility_requirements
steps.instruction: Define accessibility requirements based on regulatory obligations and best practices. Output: target_standard (WCAG 2.1 AA minimum), specific_requirements organized by WCAG principle: perceivable (text alternatives, captions, contrast ratios 4.5:1 normal / 3:1 large, resize to 200%, meaningful sequence), operable (keyboard accessible, no keyboard traps, timing adjustable, no seizure-inducing content, skip navigation, focus visible, touch target minimum 44x44px), understandable (language declared, predictable navigation, input assistance, error identification), robust (valid HTML, name/role/value for custom components, status messages). For each: req_id, WCAG criterion reference, implementation guidance, testing method (automated vs manual).
steps.done_when: accessibility_requirements exists with WCAG-referenced requirements, each with implementation and testing guidance.

steps.name: platform_requirements
steps.instruction: Define platform and device support requirements. Based on audience_research_data, determine: primary_platform (web, iOS, Android, desktop — which first?), browser_support (list specific browsers and minimum versions, recommend: Chrome last 2, Firefox last 2, Safari last 2, Edge last 2), mobile_support (responsive web, PWA, native app — which approach?), screen_sizes (minimum supported width, key breakpoints), os_versions (minimum iOS version, minimum Android version if applicable), device_types (phone, tablet, desktop, specific devices to test on). If the audience data suggests multiple platforms are equally important, trigger needs_clarification to ask human which platform to prioritize for MVP.
steps.done_when: platform_requirements exists with specific platform, browser, and device support definitions.

steps.name: internationalization_requirements
steps.instruction: Define internationalization requirements. Based on target audience geography: initial_languages (list languages for MVP, typically just English), future_languages (planned), rtl_support_needed (Arabic, Hebrew), date_format_handling (ISO 8601 internal, locale-aware display), number_format_handling (decimal separator, thousands separator), currency_handling (supported currencies, display format), timezone_handling (UTC storage, user-local display, timezone selector), text_expansion_budget (UI must accommodate 30-40% text expansion for translation), string_extraction_strategy (all user-facing strings externalized from day 1 even if only one language). Even if MVP is English-only, the architecture must support i18n from the start.
steps.done_when: i18n_requirements exists defining language support strategy and architectural requirements for future localization.

steps.name: business_rules_extraction
steps.instruction: Extract every business rule from prd_final and monetization_strategy. A business rule is a constraint on data or behavior. For each: rule_id (BR-001...), title, rule_statement (precise, unambiguous: 'IF condition THEN consequence'), category (validation, authorization, limit, calculation, workflow, billing), source (which document/section), affected_features (which features enforce this rule), testable_assertion (how to verify this rule holds). Examples: 'BR-001: Free tier users can create maximum 5 projects', 'BR-007: Passwords must be minimum 8 characters with at least one number and one special character', 'BR-015: Subscription downgrades take effect at end of current billing period'.
steps.done_when: business_rules exists as a numbered list of precise, testable business rule statements.

steps.name: acceptance_criteria_audit
steps.instruction: Audit all user story acceptance criteria. For each user story, check: 1) Has at least one Given/When/Then criterion, 2) Happy path is covered, 3) At least one error/edge case is covered, 4) Criteria are specific (no 'should work correctly'), 5) Criteria are automatable (can be turned into a test), 6) Relevant business rules are reflected, 7) No ambiguous terms ('quickly', 'properly', 'appropriate'). Output: stories_passed (list of story IDs that pass all checks), stories_failed (list with: story_id, issues found, suggested fixes). Also flag any functional_requirements or business_rules that have no corresponding acceptance criteria anywhere.
steps.done_when: acceptance_criteria_audit_result exists with pass/fail per story and suggestions for every failed story.

steps.name: acceptance_criteria_fixes
steps.instruction: For every story in acceptance_criteria_audit_result.stories_failed, rewrite or add acceptance criteria to address all flagged issues. Use Given/When/Then format. Ensure every criterion is: specific (exact values, not 'appropriate'), testable (an automated test can verify), complete (covers the scenario fully). For functional_requirements or business_rules with no acceptance criteria, add criteria to the most relevant user story or create a new technical story. Output the complete updated user_stories_refined (all stories, not just changed ones).
steps.done_when: user_stories_refined exists with all acceptance criteria issues resolved.

steps.name: requirements_traceability_matrix
steps.instruction: Create a requirements traceability matrix. This is a structured mapping: functional_requirement_id → user_story_ids → business_rule_ids → test_case_ids (placeholder 'TBD' for now). Every FR must map to at least one user story. Every business rule must map to at least one user story. Every user story must map to at least one FR. Flag any orphans: FRs with no stories, stories with no FRs, business rules with no stories. Output as a structured table/JSON.
steps.done_when: traceability_matrix exists with all requirements cross-referenced and no orphans (or orphans explicitly justified).

steps.name: requirements_conflict_detection
steps.instruction: Scan all requirements for conflicts, contradictions, or impossible combinations. Check: 1) FR vs FR conflicts (two features that can't coexist), 2) FR vs NFR conflicts (feature requires behavior that violates performance targets), 3) NFR vs NFR conflicts (availability target conflicts with cost constraint), 4) Business rule conflicts (two rules that contradict), 5) Security vs usability conflicts (security requirement makes UX unacceptable), 6) Scale vs cost conflicts. For each conflict: conflict_id, requirement_ids involved, nature of conflict, severity (blocking/significant/minor), suggested_resolution options.
steps.done_when: requirements_conflicts exists listing all detected conflicts with severity and resolution options.

steps.name: requirements_conflict_resolution
steps.instruction: For each conflict in requirements_conflicts: if severity is 'minor', choose the most reasonable resolution and document it. If severity is 'significant', choose the resolution that best serves the product vision and document the trade-off. If severity is 'blocking', trigger needs_clarification presenting the conflict, options, and trade-offs to the human for decision. Output: resolution_id, conflict_id, chosen_resolution, trade_off_accepted, requirements_to_update (list of req_ids and how they change).
steps.done_when: conflict_resolutions exists with every conflict resolved and all blocking conflicts cleared with human input.

steps.name: requirements_specification_document
steps.instruction: Compile all requirements into a structured Requirements Specification document. Sections: 1) Functional Requirements (grouped by category), 2) Non-Functional Requirements (performance, scalability, availability), 3) Security Requirements, 4) Data Requirements, 5) Integration Requirements, 6) Accessibility Requirements, 7) Platform Requirements, 8) Internationalization Requirements, 9) Business Rules, 10) User Stories (refined, with acceptance criteria), 11) Traceability Matrix, 12) Resolved Conflicts. Add version number, table of contents, and glossary of terms.
steps.done_when: requirements_spec exists as a comprehensive versioned document incorporating all requirement categories.

steps.name: architecture_pattern_evaluation
steps.instruction: Evaluate architecture patterns against the requirements. Patterns to evaluate: monolith (single deployable unit), modular_monolith (single deployable, internal module boundaries), microservices (independent services per domain), serverless (function-as-a-service), event_driven (event bus with consumers), hybrid (e.g., monolith with serverless functions for specific tasks). For each pattern: description, pros (for our specific requirements), cons (for our specific requirements), fits_scale_requirements (yes/no with explanation), fits_team_size (yes/no — consider this is AI-built), implementation_complexity (low/medium/high), operational_complexity (low/medium/high), migration_path (can we start here and evolve?). Recommend the top 2 patterns.
steps.done_when: architecture_pattern_evaluation exists with all patterns evaluated and top 2 recommended.

steps.name: architecture_pattern_selection
steps.instruction: Select the architecture pattern. If the top 2 recommended patterns have significantly different trade-offs (e.g., one is faster to build but harder to scale, the other is harder to build but scales easily), trigger needs_clarification presenting both options with clear trade-off summary (build speed vs scalability, operational complexity vs flexibility) and ask human to choose. If one pattern is clearly superior, select it with justification. Output: selected_pattern, justification, key_constraints this pattern imposes, migration_path_to_next_pattern (how we evolve when we outgrow this).
steps.done_when: architecture_pattern_decision exists with the selected pattern and clear justification.

steps.name: frontend_technology_research
steps.instruction: Research frontend framework options. Evaluate: React (Next.js, Vite+React), Vue (Nuxt), Svelte (SvelteKit), Angular, Solid. For each: github_stars, npm_weekly_downloads, latest_release_date, major_version_stability, learning_curve, ecosystem_size (UI libraries, state management, tooling), performance_benchmarks (bundle size, runtime performance), ssr_support, mobile_story (React Native, Capacitor, etc.), typescript_support_quality, community_size, job_market_relevance, testing_ecosystem. Also evaluate meta-frameworks: Next.js vs Remix vs Nuxt vs SvelteKit. Present as a structured comparison. Do not recommend — just present data.
steps.done_when: frontend_tech_research exists with structured comparison data for all major frontend options.

steps.name: backend_technology_research
steps.instruction: Research backend framework options. Evaluate: Node.js (Express, Fastify, NestJS, Hono), Python (FastAPI, Django), Go (Gin, Echo, Fiber), Rust (Axum, Actix), Ruby (Rails), Elixir (Phoenix). For each: performance_benchmarks (requests per second, latency), memory_usage, ecosystem_maturity, orm_options, authentication_libraries, testing_frameworks, deployment_simplicity, docker_image_size, cold_start_time (for serverless), ai_code_generation_quality (how well do LLMs generate code in this language — critical for our AI-agent workflow), type_safety, concurrency_model. Present as structured comparison without recommendation.
steps.done_when: backend_tech_research exists with structured comparison emphasizing AI-generation quality alongside traditional metrics.

steps.name: database_technology_research
steps.instruction: Research database options based on data_requirements. Evaluate: PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB, SQLite (for simple products), PlanetScale (MySQL), Supabase (Postgres), Neon (Postgres), CockroachDB, Redis (as primary for specific use cases). For each: data_model_fit (relational vs document vs key-value for our data), scalability_path (read replicas, sharding, serverless scaling), managed_service_options (with pricing at our expected scale), backup_and_recovery (built-in features), full_text_search (built-in vs needs separate service), json_support (for flexible schema needs), migration_tooling, connection_pooling_options, community_and_tooling. Present structured comparison.
steps.done_when: database_tech_research exists with structured comparison matched to our data requirements.

steps.name: infrastructure_research
steps.instruction: Research hosting and infrastructure options. Evaluate: AWS (ECS/EKS, Lambda, RDS), GCP (Cloud Run, GKE, Cloud SQL), Azure, Vercel (frontend), Railway, Render, Fly.io, Hetzner, DigitalOcean. For each: pricing_at_our_scale (estimate monthly cost for MVP traffic levels), auto_scaling_capability, deployment_complexity (how hard to set up CI/CD), managed_services_available (database, cache, storage, CDN, email), multi_region_support, free_tier (for development/staging), vendor_lock_in_level (low/medium/high), disaster_recovery_features. Also evaluate: container orchestration needs (Docker only, Docker Compose, Kubernetes, or managed containers).
steps.done_when: infrastructure_research exists with hosting options compared including cost estimates.

steps.name: technology_stack_selection
steps.instruction: Select the complete technology stack. For each layer, recommend a specific choice with justification: frontend_framework (framework + meta-framework), frontend_language (TypeScript), backend_framework (specific framework), backend_language, database_primary, database_cache (Redis or alternative), object_storage (S3/R2/etc), hosting_platform, container_strategy, ci_cd_platform (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, etc). For each choice: selected_option, justification (3-5 bullet points), risks, alternatives_considered. Weight AI-code-generation quality heavily since agents will write all code. This is a major commitment — trigger needs_clarification to present the stack and get human approval.
steps.done_when: tech_stack_decision exists with every layer specified, justified, and approved by human.

steps.name: system_architecture_design
steps.instruction: Design the high-level system architecture. Output a detailed description of: components (list each service/module with responsibility), databases (each database instance and what it stores), external_services (each third-party integration point), communication_patterns (sync HTTP, async queue, WebSocket — which components use which), data_flow (how a request flows through the system for key operations), network_topology (load balancer, CDN, application servers, database, cache — how they connect), environments (development, staging, production — what differs). Describe the architecture in enough detail that a text-to-diagram tool could generate an architecture diagram. Use consistent naming for all components.
steps.done_when: system_architecture exists with all components, their interactions, data flows, and network topology fully described.

steps.name: database_schema_design
steps.instruction: Design the complete database schema. For each table/collection: table_name, description, columns (name, type, nullable, default, constraints), primary_key, unique_constraints, indexes (columns, type: btree/gin/gist, purpose), foreign_keys (references, on_delete behavior), enums (if applicable). Include: standard columns (id, created_at, updated_at, deleted_at for soft delete if needed), audit columns if required by security_requirements. Design for: normalized form (3NF minimum for relational), query efficiency (indexes for known access patterns from data_requirements), referential integrity, soft delete where business requires it. Also define: database-level constraints, check constraints for business rules that can be enforced at DB level.
steps.done_when: database_schema exists with every table, column, index, constraint, and relationship fully specified.

steps.name: database_schema_review
steps.instruction: Review the database schema for: 1) Normalization (no redundant data unless intentionally denormalized with justification), 2) Missing indexes for known query patterns, 3) N+1 query risks (relationships that will be queried together should have supporting indexes), 4) Missing constraints (nullable columns that shouldn't be, missing foreign keys), 5) Scalability concerns (tables that will grow large — do they have appropriate partitioning strategy?), 6) Naming consistency (snake_case, plural table names, consistent id naming), 7) Missing audit/timestamp columns, 8) Soft delete consistency, 9) Enum handling, 10) Business rules enforceable at DB level but missing. Output: status, issues with table/column references and fix suggestions.
steps.done_when: schema_review_result exists with thorough schema assessment.

steps.name: api_resource_modeling
steps.instruction: Define the API resource model. For each resource: resource_name (plural, lowercase), description, corresponding_database_table(s), base_url_path (e.g., /api/v1/projects), sub_resources (nested resources, e.g., /projects/:id/tasks), relationships (to other resources), filterable_fields, sortable_fields, searchable_fields. Define: api_versioning_strategy (URL path /v1/ recommended), pagination_strategy (cursor-based for large collections, offset for small), response_envelope_format (standard wrapper: {data, meta, errors}), id_format (UUID v4 recommended for security, or nanoid for shorter URLs).
steps.done_when: api_resource_model exists with all resources, their relationships, URL structure, and API conventions defined.

steps.name: api_endpoint_design
steps.instruction: Design every API endpoint. For each: endpoint_id (EP-001...), method (GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE), path, description, authentication_required (yes/no), required_role (if role-restricted), path_parameters (name, type, validation), query_parameters (name, type, required, default, validation), request_body_schema (JSON schema with field types, required fields, validation rules), response_schema_success (JSON schema with status code), response_schema_errors (list of possible error responses with status codes and error body), rate_limit (requests per minute for this endpoint), idempotency (is the operation idempotent? idempotency key needed?), side_effects (emails sent, events emitted, jobs queued). Every functional requirement should map to at least one endpoint.
steps.done_when: api_endpoints exists with every endpoint fully specified including request/response schemas and error cases.

steps.name: api_specification_document
steps.instruction: Convert api_endpoints into a formal OpenAPI 3.1 specification document. Include: info (title, version, description), servers (development, staging, production URLs), paths (every endpoint with parameters, request bodies, responses), components/schemas (reusable data models for request/response bodies), components/securitySchemes (JWT Bearer, API Key, OAuth2 as applicable), tags (grouping endpoints by resource). Ensure every endpoint has: summary, description, operationId, all parameters, request body (if applicable), all response codes (200/201/204 for success, 400/401/403/404/409/422/429/500 as applicable), example values for request and response bodies.
steps.done_when: openapi_spec exists as a valid OpenAPI 3.1 document covering all endpoints with examples.

steps.name: authentication_design
steps.instruction: Design the authentication system. Specify: registration_flow (email/password: validation rules, email verification mechanism, verification token expiry), oauth_flow (which providers, account linking strategy when same email exists, data we request from provider), login_flow (credential validation, brute force protection: max attempts, lockout duration, progressive delay), session_strategy (JWT recommended: access token expiry 15min, refresh token expiry 7-30 days, token storage: httpOnly cookie vs localStorage trade-offs), refresh_mechanism (silent refresh flow, refresh token rotation, reuse detection), logout_flow (token invalidation, refresh token revocation, all-devices logout), password_reset_flow (token generation, expiry 1hr, single-use, email delivery), password_hashing (algorithm: argon2id or bcrypt, parameters), mfa_design (if required: TOTP, SMS, backup codes). Include sequence diagrams described in text for each flow.
steps.done_when: auth_design exists with every auth flow fully specified including token strategy, security parameters, and flow sequences.

steps.name: authorization_design
steps.instruction: Design the authorization system. Specify: access_control_model (RBAC recommended for MVP — roles with permissions), roles (list each role: name, description, permission set), permissions (list each permission: name, resource, actions allowed), default_role (assigned on registration), role_hierarchy (if any: admin > moderator > user), resource_ownership (users own their resources, isolation between users/tenants), enforcement_points (API middleware: check role/permission before handler, Frontend route guards: check role before rendering, UI element visibility: show/hide based on permission), super_admin (how to create, what they can do, audit logging for admin actions), permission_checking_logic (pseudocode for the middleware that checks authorization on every request).
steps.done_when: authz_design exists with roles, permissions, enforcement points, and checking logic fully specified.

steps.name: frontend_architecture_design
steps.instruction: Design the frontend architecture. Specify: folder_structure (exact directory tree with purpose of each directory), component_hierarchy (page components, layout components, feature components, shared components, primitive components — naming convention for each), state_management_strategy (global state: what goes in global store, server state: how API data is cached/invalidated using React Query or equivalent, form state: how forms are managed, UI state: local component state), routing_structure (list all routes with: path, component, auth_required, layout, lazy_loaded), data_fetching_pattern (hooks/composables that wrap API calls, loading/error/success handling, optimistic updates where applicable), code_splitting_strategy (route-based splitting, lazy loading boundaries), error_boundary_strategy (where to catch errors, fallback UI), environment_configuration (how env vars reach the frontend, build-time vs runtime config).
steps.done_when: frontend_architecture exists with folder structure, component hierarchy, state patterns, routing, and data fetching fully specified.

steps.name: data_flow_diagrams
steps.instruction: Create detailed data flow descriptions for every major operation. For each flow: flow_name, trigger (user action or system event), steps (numbered, each step: actor, action, data_sent, data_received, error_conditions). Flows to document: user_registration, user_login, password_reset, core_feature_create (the primary value action), core_feature_read (viewing data), core_feature_update, core_feature_delete, file_upload (if applicable), payment_subscription (if applicable), notification_delivery, search_query, admin_action. Each flow should trace data from the user's browser through every system component to the database and back. Include error branches.
steps.done_when: data_flow_diagrams exists with step-by-step data flows for all major operations including error branches.

steps.name: third_party_service_selection
steps.instruction: For each integration in integration_requirements, recommend a specific vendor. For each: integration_purpose, selected_vendor, pricing_at_mvp_scale (monthly cost estimate), pricing_at_growth_scale (10x traffic), setup_complexity (low/medium/high), sdk_quality_for_our_stack (does it have a good SDK for our chosen language?), free_tier_details, alternatives_considered (and why rejected), lock_in_risk (how hard to switch later), data_residency (where data is stored — matters for GDPR). Calculate total monthly cost for all services at MVP scale and at growth scale. If total cost exceeds reasonable expectations, trigger needs_clarification presenting costs and asking human if budget is acceptable or which services to cut.
steps.done_when: third_party_selections exists with a vendor selected for each integration, with costs calculated and approved.

steps.name: caching_strategy
steps.instruction: Design the caching strategy. Specify: cache_technology (Redis recommended), caching_layers (CDN cache for static assets, application cache for API responses, database query cache), per_endpoint_caching (for each GET endpoint: should it be cached? TTL? cache key pattern? invalidation trigger?), cache_patterns (cache-aside for reads, write-through for consistency-critical data, write-behind for performance-critical writes), cache_invalidation_strategy (event-based invalidation: when entity X is updated, invalidate keys matching pattern Y), session_cache (if using server-side sessions), rate_limit_storage (Redis for rate limit counters), cache_warming (any caches that should be pre-populated on deploy?), cache_failure_handling (what happens when Redis is down: degrade gracefully, bypass cache, serve stale data?).
steps.done_when: caching_strategy exists with per-endpoint caching decisions, invalidation rules, and failure handling.

steps.name: file_storage_design
steps.instruction: Design file upload and storage. Specify: storage_service (from third_party_selections: S3, R2, etc.), upload_flow (direct-to-storage with presigned URLs recommended: client requests presigned URL from API, client uploads directly to storage, client notifies API of completion, API validates and records metadata), file_organization (bucket structure, key naming convention: /{tenant_id}/{entity_type}/{entity_id}/{filename}), file_type_validation (allowed MIME types, magic byte validation not just extension), size_limits (per file, per user quota), image_processing (thumbnails, resizing: on-upload vs on-demand via CDN transform), cdn_delivery (serve files through CDN with signed URLs if private), cleanup_strategy (orphaned files, deleted entity files), virus_scanning (if handling untrusted uploads).
steps.done_when: file_storage_design exists with upload flow, storage structure, validation, and delivery fully specified.

steps.name: realtime_design
steps.instruction: If any functional requirements need real-time updates (live notifications, collaborative editing, presence indicators, live dashboards), design the real-time system. Specify: technology_choice (WebSocket vs SSE vs long-polling, with justification), connection_management (authentication on connect, heartbeat interval, reconnection strategy with exponential backoff), channel_model (what channels/rooms exist, who can subscribe to what), event_types (list every real-time event: name, payload schema, who receives it), scaling_approach (sticky sessions, Redis pub/sub for multi-server, or managed service like Pusher/Ably), fallback_behavior (what if WebSocket connection fails: poll every N seconds). If no real-time features are needed, output a brief document stating that and recommending the simplest future path to add real-time.
steps.done_when: realtime_design exists (either full design or explicit 'not needed' with future migration path).

steps.name: background_job_design
steps.instruction: Design the background job system. Specify: technology (BullMQ/Redis, SQS, RabbitMQ, or database-backed queue — justify based on tech stack), job_types (list every background job: name, trigger, payload, processing_logic, retry_policy, timeout, priority), queue_configuration (separate queues for different priorities? dedicated workers?), retry_strategy (per job type: max_retries, backoff_type: exponential/linear, backoff_base), dead_letter_handling (where failed jobs go after max retries, alerting on DLQ), scheduled_jobs (cron jobs: name, schedule, purpose — e.g., daily cleanup, subscription renewal check, analytics aggregation), concurrency_limits (max concurrent jobs per type), monitoring (job success/failure rates, queue depth, processing time).
steps.done_when: background_job_design exists with all job types, queue configuration, retry policies, and monitoring defined.

steps.name: email_system_design
steps.instruction: Design the transactional email system. Specify: email_service (from third_party_selections), email_types (list every email the system sends: name, trigger, recipient, subject_template, required_data, priority), template_system (how templates are managed: code-based with variables, service-based template editor, or markdown-based), template_variables (common variables available in all templates: app_name, user_name, support_email, unsubscribe_link), sending_architecture (sync for critical emails like verification, async via job queue for non-critical), delivery_monitoring (bounce handling, complaint handling, delivery rate tracking), email_preferences (per-user opt-in/opt-out per email category), development_mode (intercept all emails in dev/staging, send to Mailtrap or similar), domain_authentication (SPF, DKIM, DMARC records needed).
steps.done_when: email_system_design exists with all email types, template strategy, and delivery architecture specified.

steps.name: search_system_design
steps.instruction: Design the search system if any features require search. Evaluate complexity: simple_search (database LIKE/ILIKE or full-text search: use PostgreSQL tsvector or MongoDB text index — no external service needed), medium_search (autocomplete, typo tolerance, faceted search: use Meilisearch or Typesense — lightweight, easy to operate), complex_search (multi-entity search, relevance tuning, geospatial: use Elasticsearch or Algolia). Specify: technology_choice (with justification matching our complexity level), indexed_entities (which data is searchable), index_structure (fields, weights, filterable attributes, sortable attributes), sync_strategy (how search index stays in sync with database: real-time via events, or periodic reindex), search_api_design (endpoint, query parameters, response format, highlighting, pagination).
steps.done_when: search_system_design exists with technology choice justified and index/sync strategy defined.

steps.name: error_handling_strategy
steps.instruction: Design the global error handling strategy. Specify: error_response_format ({error: {code: string, message: string, details: object[], request_id: string}}), error_code_taxonomy (hierarchical: AUTH_001, VALIDATION_002, RESOURCE_001, RATE_LIMIT_001, INTERNAL_001 — list all codes with meanings), http_status_mapping (which error codes map to which HTTP status), error_logging_rules (what to log: stack trace, request context, user id — what NOT to log: passwords, tokens, PII in error details), client_error_handling (frontend error boundary strategy, toast vs inline error display rules, retry behavior per error type), validation_error_format (field-level errors: {field: string, code: string, message: string}[]), unhandled_error_behavior (catch-all: log, return generic 500, alert if rate spikes), third_party_error_handling (how to handle downstream service failures: retry, circuit breaker, fallback).
steps.done_when: error_handling_strategy exists with error taxonomy, response format, logging rules, and circuit breaker policies.

steps.name: logging_strategy
steps.instruction: Design the structured logging strategy. Specify: log_format (JSON structured: {timestamp, level, message, request_id, user_id, service, action, duration_ms, metadata}), log_levels (debug: detailed development info, info: normal operations, warn: recoverable issues, error: failures requiring attention, fatal: system cannot continue), per_request_logging (log every API request: method, path, status_code, duration_ms, user_id, request_id), correlation_id_propagation (how request_id flows through all services and async jobs), pii_scrubbing (fields to redact: password, token, credit_card, ssn — scrub before logging), log_retention (development: 7 days, staging: 30 days, production: 90 days), log_aggregation_service (from infrastructure choice), search_and_alerting (log-based alerts: error rate threshold, specific error patterns).
steps.done_when: logging_strategy exists with format, levels, PII rules, retention, and aggregation fully specified.

steps.name: monitoring_strategy
steps.instruction: Design the monitoring and alerting strategy. Specify: health_check_endpoints (per service: what they check, expected response), key_metrics (RED metrics per service: Rate, Errors, Duration; USE metrics per resource: Utilization, Saturation, Errors), application_metrics (custom: signup_rate, active_users, queue_depth, cache_hit_rate), infrastructure_metrics (CPU, memory, disk, network per service), alert_rules (for each metric: condition, threshold, duration, severity, notification_channel), alert_severity_levels (critical: pages on-call / sends Telegram immediately, warning: sends Telegram during business hours, info: logged to dashboard only), dashboards (list dashboards to create: system_overview, api_performance, error_tracking, business_metrics, infrastructure), on_call_process (how alerts are handled: who is notified, escalation path).
steps.done_when: monitoring_strategy exists with metrics, alert rules, severity levels, and dashboard definitions.

steps.name: scalability_plan
steps.instruction: Document how each component scales to meet nfr_scalability targets. For each component: component_name, current_capacity_estimate, scaling_type (horizontal: add instances, vertical: bigger instance, or auto: managed scaling), scaling_trigger (CPU > 70%, request queue > 100, etc.), max_scale_limit, database_scaling (read replicas for read-heavy, connection pooling limits, query optimization checkpoints, sharding plan if needed in future), cache_scaling (Redis cluster vs single instance, memory limits), cost_at_scale (estimated monthly cost at each scale tier). Define scaling milestones: 100 users, 1K users, 10K users, 100K users — what changes are needed at each level.
steps.done_when: scalability_plan exists with per-component scaling strategy and milestone-based evolution plan.

steps.name: disaster_recovery_plan
steps.instruction: Design the disaster recovery plan. Specify: backup_strategy (database: automated daily snapshots + continuous WAL archiving if Postgres, retention: 30 days, file_storage: versioning enabled, cross-region replication if critical), restore_procedure (step-by-step: how to restore database from backup, verify integrity, update DNS if needed), rto_target (from nfr_availability), rpo_target (from nfr_availability), failure_scenarios (for each: database_corruption, complete_server_failure, region_outage, dependency_outage, data_breach, accidental_deletion — response procedure for each), restore_testing (monthly backup restore test to verify backups work), multi_region_strategy (if required by availability targets: active-passive vs active-active, data replication approach), incident_communication (how users are notified during outage: status page, email, in-app banner).
steps.done_when: disaster_recovery_plan exists with backup strategy, restore procedures, and per-scenario response plans.

steps.name: security_architecture_review
steps.instruction: Review the entire architecture for security. Check: 1) Authentication: are all endpoints protected? Are tokens stored securely? Is refresh rotation implemented? 2) Authorization: is every resource access checked? IDOR prevention? 3) Data protection: encryption at rest and in transit for all sensitive data? 4) Network: is the database publicly accessible? Are internal services exposed? 5) Secrets: where are API keys stored? Are they in code? 6) Attack surface: what's exposed to the internet? Minimum necessary? 7) Dependency risk: are we dependent on services with poor security track records? 8) File uploads: validated and sandboxed? 9) Rate limiting: applied at all entry points? 10) Logging: are we logging security events? Not logging sensitive data? Output: findings with severity (critical/high/medium/low) and remediation recommendations.
steps.done_when: security_architecture_review_result exists with all findings categorized by severity.

steps.name: architecture_decision_records
steps.instruction: Write Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) for every significant decision made in this phase. For each ADR: adr_id (ADR-001...), title, date, status (accepted), context (what situation required a decision), options_considered (list each option with pros and cons), decision (what was chosen), consequences (positive and negative outcomes of this decision), participants (which agents/human were involved). Minimum ADRs required: architecture pattern, frontend framework, backend framework, database choice, hosting platform, authentication approach, each third-party vendor. ADRs are append-only — they are never modified, only superseded by new ADRs.
steps.done_when: adrs collection exists with an ADR for every major architectural decision.

steps.name: technical_design_document
steps.instruction: Compile all architecture artifacts into a single Technical Design Document (TDD). Structure: 1) System Overview (architecture diagram description, tech stack summary), 2) Component Architecture (each service/module), 3) Database Design (schema, access patterns), 4) API Design (resource model, endpoint summary, link to full OpenAPI spec), 5) Authentication & Authorization, 6) Frontend Architecture, 7) Data Flows, 8) Caching, 9) File Storage, 10) Real-time (if applicable), 11) Background Jobs, 12) Email System, 13) Search, 14) Error Handling, 15) Logging & Monitoring, 16) Scalability, 17) Disaster Recovery, 18) Security Considerations, 19) ADRs. Add table of contents and version number.
steps.done_when: technical_design_document exists as a comprehensive reference for all implementation work.

steps.name: architecture_review
steps.instruction: Final review of the technical design. Check: 1) Every functional requirement is addressable by the architecture, 2) All NFRs have corresponding design elements, 3) No single points of failure for critical paths, 4) Security review findings from 4.30 have been addressed, 5) Consistency across all sections (same naming, same patterns), 6) Feasibility (can this actually be built with the chosen stack?), 7) Over-engineering check (is the architecture appropriately complex for an MVP, or are we building for scale we don't need yet?), 8) Missing pieces (any system aspect not covered?). Output: status (approved/needs_revision), issues, commendations (what's done well).
steps.done_when: architecture_review_result exists with comprehensive assessment of the technical design.

steps.name: information_architecture
steps.instruction: Define the app's information architecture.
