Examples: file upload middleware (multer or equivalent configured with size limits and type validation), specific rate limiting rules, feature-flag checks, request preprocessing. Follow the same patterns as base middleware from Sprint 0. Middleware should be reusable where possible (parameterized, not hardcoded to one route).

steps.name: backend_unit_tests
steps.instruction: Write unit tests for the service layer of feature '{feature_name}'. For each service function, test: happy_path (valid input produces expected output), validation_failures (each invalid input type returns appropriate error), authorization_failures (unauthorized user gets forbidden error), business_rule_enforcement (each business rule from feature_spec_summary is tested: boundary values, violations), edge_cases (empty input, maximum length, special characters, concurrent operations if applicable), error_handling (database errors are caught and wrapped in AppError). Mock external dependencies (database, email service, storage) to test business logic in isolation. Aim for >90% branch coverage of the service file. Use descriptive test names: 'should [expected behavior] when [condition]'.

steps.name: backend_integration_tests
steps.instruction: Write integration tests for the API endpoints of feature '{feature_name}'. Test each endpoint with a real test database (no mocking). For each endpoint test: successful_request (correct status code, response body matches openapi_spec schema, database state changed correctly), authentication_required (request without token returns 401), authorization (request from wrong role returns 403), validation_errors (invalid body returns 422 with field-level errors), not_found (request for non-existent resource returns 404), conflict (duplicate creation returns 409 if applicable), pagination (if list endpoint: verify page size, total count, next/prev links), filtering_and_sorting (if supported: verify each filter and sort option). Use test helpers: createTestUser, authenticatedRequest. Clean database between tests.

steps.name: frontend_types
steps.instruction: Define TypeScript types for feature '{feature_name}'. Create src/types/{feature_name}.ts: API response types (matching openapi_spec response schemas exactly), API request types (matching request body schemas), form types (form field values, may differ from API types — e.g., date as string in form, Date object in API), state types (what's stored in the client state for this feature), component prop types (for feature-specific components). Ensure: response types match what the API actually returns, form types include validation state, all types are exported for use in components and API client.

steps.name: frontend_state
steps.instruction: Set up client-side state management for feature '{feature_name}'. If using server-state library (React Query/SWR): create query hooks (useFeatureList, useFeatureDetail, etc.) with: query key convention, stale time, error handling. Create mutation hooks (useCreateFeature, useUpdateFeature, useDeleteFeature) with: optimistic updates where appropriate, cache invalidation on success, error rollback. If using client-state store: create store slice with: state shape, actions, selectors. Follow existing patterns in the codebase. Each hook should handle loading, error, and success states and return typed data.

steps.name: frontend_api_client
steps.instruction: Create API client functions for feature '{feature_name}'. Create src/api/{feature_name}.ts: one function per API endpoint (e.g., getFeatures, getFeatureById, createFeature, updateFeature, deleteFeature). Each function: uses the base API client from Sprint 0, accepts typed parameters, returns typed response (Promise<ApiResponse<FeatureType>>), handles query parameter construction for list endpoints (pagination, filters, sorting). Follow the existing API client patterns. These functions are called by the state management hooks from the previous step.

steps.name: frontend_components
steps.instruction: Implement feature-specific UI components for '{feature_name}' per screen_specifications. For each new component: follow the component tree from screen_specifications exactly, use primitive and composite components from the design system (Button, Input, Card, etc.), apply design tokens for all styling (no hardcoded colors, spacing, or font sizes), accept typed props, handle all visual states (default, hover, focus, disabled, loading). Create in src/components/features/{feature_name}/. Each component should be self-contained and testable. If a component will be reused across features, place it in src/components/composite/ instead.

steps.name: frontend_page_assembly
steps.instruction: Assemble feature '{feature_name}' pages per screen_specifications. For each page: create in src/pages/ or src/app/ (per framework routing convention), import and compose feature components, wire data_bindings (connect state/API data to component props), wire action_handlers (connect component events to mutations/navigation/state changes), implement conditional_rendering (show/hide elements based on state, role, data presence), implement route parameters (extract IDs from URL, fetch relevant data), implement navigation (links to related pages, breadcrumbs, back navigation). Each page should: fetch its data on mount, show loading state while fetching, show error state if fetch fails, show empty state if no data, show content when data is available.

steps.name: frontend_form_handling
steps.condition: Only execute if feature has forms
steps.instruction: Implement form handling for feature '{feature_name}' per form_specs. For each form: use form library (React Hook Form, Formik, or native — per project convention), implement field validation matching business_rules (real-time validation on blur, not just on submit), display error messages per field using the Input component's errorMessage prop, implement submit handling (disable button, show loading, call API mutation, on success: show toast + navigate or reset, on error: show field-level errors from API response or toast for general errors), implement unsaved changes detection (warn on navigation away if form is dirty), implement auto-save if specified in form_specs. Handle: multi-step forms (save progress between steps), dynamic fields (add/remove items), file upload fields (progress indicator).

steps.name: frontend_loading_error_empty
steps.instruction: Implement loading, error, and empty states for all screens in feature '{feature_name}'. Loading: use Skeleton components matching the layout of the content they replace (not a centered spinner), show skeleton immediately, transition smoothly to content when data arrives. Error: show inline error message with retry button for data fetch failures, show toast for action failures (create/update/delete), include the request_id in error display for debugging. Empty: use EmptyState component with appropriate illustration_description, heading, body text, and CTA from empty_error_state_specs. Implement: retry logic (exponential backoff for automatic retry, manual retry button for user-triggered), offline detection (show banner when offline, queue actions for when back online if applicable).

steps.name: frontend_responsive
steps.instruction: Implement responsive behavior for feature '{feature_name}' screens per responsive_spec. At each breakpoint: apply layout changes (column stacking, sidebar collapse, grid column count changes), apply hidden elements (elements marked as hidden on mobile), apply component swaps (table → card list on mobile, horizontal tabs → select dropdown), ensure touch targets are minimum 44x44px on mobile, ensure text remains readable (no text smaller than 14px on mobile). Test: resize browser through all breakpoints, verify no horizontal overflow, verify no overlapping elements, verify all interactive elements are reachable. Use CSS media queries or framework-specific responsive utilities (Tailwind responsive prefixes, CSS Container Queries).

steps.name: frontend_accessibility
steps.instruction: Implement accessibility for feature '{feature_name}' per accessibility_spec. For each screen in this feature: set correct heading hierarchy (h1 → h2 → h3, no skipped levels), add ARIA labels to all interactive elements lacking visible text (icon buttons, image links), add aria-live regions for dynamic content (search results count, form validation messages), implement focus_order per accessibility_spec (set tabindex where natural order is wrong), implement keyboard interaction for custom components (arrow keys for menus, space/enter for buttons), add skip links if this feature adds new navigation, ensure all images have alt text (decorative: alt='', informative: descriptive text), ensure form fields have associated labels (visible or aria-label). Run axe accessibility checker on each page and fix all violations.

steps.name: frontend_unit_tests
steps.instruction: Write frontend unit tests for feature '{feature_name}'. Test: utility functions (formatters, validators, transformers — pure function tests), custom hooks (render hook, verify return values and state changes), state management (actions dispatch correctly, selectors return expected values, mutations invalidate correct queries). Do not test components here (that's the next step). Use the testing utilities from Sprint 0 setup. Aim for 100% coverage of utility functions and hooks.

steps.name: frontend_component_tests
steps.instruction: Write component tests for feature '{feature_name}'. For each feature-specific component: render test (renders without crashing, displays expected content), interaction test (click buttons, fill inputs, toggle checkboxes — verify state changes and callbacks), conditional rendering test (different props or state → different visible elements), accessibility test (has correct ARIA attributes, keyboard interaction works). For each page component: render with mocked API data (verify correct content displayed), render in loading state (verify skeletons shown), render in error state (verify error message and retry button), render in empty state (verify empty state component shown). Use @testing-library/react and @testing-library/user-event for realistic user interactions. Avoid testing implementation details (no checking internal state, only what the user sees).

steps.name: e2e_test
steps.instruction: Write end-to-end test(s) for feature '{feature_name}' using Playwright or Cypress. Test the complete user flow in a real browser against a running backend + database. For the primary flow: start from a realistic entry point (e.g., login → navigate to feature → perform core action → verify result). Test: happy path end-to-end (create something, verify it appears in the list, edit it, verify changes, delete it, verify removed), error handling (submit invalid data, verify error messages appear and are correct), authentication integration (verify unauthorized access redirects, verify correct data for logged-in user). Use test fixtures: seed a known database state before tests, clean up after. Use realistic selectors (data-testid attributes, accessible names — not CSS classes). Each E2E test should complete in under 30 seconds.

steps.name: run_tests
steps.instruction: Execute the full test suite to verify feature '{feature_name}' implementation. Run in order: 1) Backend unit tests (npm run test:unit), 2) Backend integration tests (npm run test:integration), 3) Frontend unit tests (npm run test:ui), 4) Frontend component tests, 5) E2E tests (npm run test:e2e). Record results: total tests run, passed, failed, skipped, coverage percentage. If all pass: proceed to next step. If any fail: output the failure details (test name, error message, stack trace, file location) for the fixer agent.

steps.name: fix_failing_tests
steps.condition: Only execute if test_results contains failures
steps.instruction: Fix all failing tests from test_results for feature '{feature_name}'. For each failure: read the test name, error message, and stack trace. Determine: is the test wrong (testing incorrect expectation) or is the implementation wrong (code has a bug)? If implementation bug: fix the source code, not the test. If test is wrong (expectation doesn't match spec): fix the test to match the specification. After fixing: re-run only the previously failing tests to verify fix. If new failures appear (regression): fix those too. Iterate until all tests pass. Output: fixes_applied (list of files changed and what was fixed), test_results_after_fix.

steps.name: code_review
steps.instruction: Review all code written for feature '{feature_name}'. Review criteria: 1) Architecture compliance (code follows the patterns defined in technical_design_document: correct layer separation, no business logic in routes, no DB calls in components), 2) Code quality (readable, well-named, DRY, single responsibility per function, no functions over 50 lines), 3) Error handling (all errors caught, user-facing errors are helpful, internal errors are logged), 4) Security (no SQL injection, XSS prevention, auth checks on every endpoint, no sensitive data in client, input validation complete), 5) Performance (no N+1 queries, appropriate indexes used, no unnecessary re-renders in frontend, images optimized), 6) Test quality (tests are meaningful, not just coverage padding, edge cases covered, tests are not brittle), 7) Type safety (no 'any' types, strict null checks), 8) Accessibility (ARIA attributes present, keyboard support, semantic HTML). Output: status (approved/changes_requested), issues (categorized by severity: blocking/suggestion, with file and line references).

steps.name: apply_review_feedback
steps.instruction: Apply all review feedback for feature '{feature_name}'. For each blocking issue in code_review_result: apply the fix. For each suggestion: apply if it improves the code without significant time cost. After applying fixes: re-run relevant tests to verify fixes don't break anything. Output: changes_applied (list), issues_deferred (suggestions not applied, with justification), tests_still_passing (boolean).

steps.name: documentation_update
steps.instruction: Update documentation for feature '{feature_name}'. Updates needed: 1) API documentation (if new endpoints: verify they appear in OpenAPI spec or update manually), 2) README (if feature adds new setup steps, environment variables, or commands), 3) Developer docs (if feature introduces new patterns or conventions). Do NOT write user-facing documentation yet (that's Phase 11). Focus only on developer-facing docs that someone working on the codebase would need.

steps.name: deploy_to_staging
steps.instruction: Deploy feature '{feature_name}' to staging. Execute: merge feature branch to develop (or trigger deployment pipeline), wait for CI pipeline to pass, wait for CD pipeline to deploy to staging, verify deployment succeeded (health check returns 200, new version is live). If deployment fails: capture error logs, output failure details for error_recovery agent. Output: deployment_status, staging_url, deployed_version.

steps.name: staging_visual_check
steps.instruction: Visually verify feature '{feature_name}' on staging. Take screenshots (or describe what is visible) of every screen in this feature at desktop and mobile breakpoints. Compare against screen_specifications: 1) Component usage (correct components used?), 2) Layout (correct spacing, alignment?), 3) Typography (correct sizes, weights?), 4) Colors (correct token usage?), 5) States (loading, empty, error states render correctly?), 6) Responsive (mobile layout matches responsive_spec?). Output: status (approved/needs_fixes), visual_issues (list with: screen, description, severity, screenshot reference).

steps.name: fix_visual_issues
steps.instruction: Fix visual issues flagged in visual_review_result for feature '{feature_name}'. For each issue: identify the component or style causing the discrepancy, apply the fix using design tokens (never hardcoded values), verify the fix at all breakpoints (don't fix desktop and break mobile). Re-run frontend tests after fixes to verify no regressions. Output: fixes_applied, re-deploy needed (boolean).

steps.name: staging_smoke_test
steps.instruction: Smoke test feature '{feature_name}' on staging. Execute the primary user flow manually: navigate to the feature, perform the core action (create/read/update/delete as applicable), verify data persists (refresh and check), test error case (submit invalid data, verify error), test auth (access without login, verify redirect). Verify: no console errors in browser, no 500 errors in API logs, no visual glitches. Output: smoke_test_passed (boolean), issues_found (list). If critical issues: output details for fixer agent. If passed: feature is complete.

steps.name: feature_completion
steps.instruction: Mark feature '{feature_name}' as complete. Verify all previous steps passed or were resolved: tests passing, code review approved, visual review approved, staging smoke test passed. Update feature_backlog: set status to 'completed'. Check: are all features in the current sprint now complete? If yes: trigger sprint_boundary_check. If no: orchestrator should proceed to the next feature in the backlog. Output: feature_status ('completed'), next_action ('next_feature' or 'sprint_boundary').

steps.name: coverage_analysis
steps.instruction: Run code coverage tools across the entire codebase. Generate coverage report: per-file line coverage, branch coverage, function coverage. Identify files below 80% line coverage and below 70% branch coverage. Prioritize uncovered areas by criticality: auth, payment, data mutation, and business rule enforcement code must have highest coverage. Output: coverage_report, gap_list (ordered by criticality).

steps.name: critical_path_test_gaps
steps.instruction: Write tests to fill coverage gaps in critical paths identified in coverage_report. Focus on: authentication flows (every auth endpoint, token refresh, session expiry), authorization (every role × every endpoint combination), payment flows (subscription create, update, cancel, webhook handling), data integrity (cascade deletes, unique constraints, referential integrity). Each test should target a specific uncovered branch or line. Run coverage after writing to verify gaps are filled.

steps.name: negative_scenario_tests
steps.instruction: Write tests for negative and error scenarios across all features. Test: invalid inputs (wrong types, missing required fields, exceeding max length, SQL injection strings, XSS payloads, null bytes), unauthorized access (every endpoint without auth token, every endpoint with expired token, every endpoint with wrong role), resource conflicts (create duplicate unique values, edit deleted resource, edit stale resource with old version), rate limiting (exceed rate limit, verify 429 response with retry-after header), request size limits (oversized body, oversized file upload), malformed requests (invalid JSON, wrong content-type, missing content-type). Each test should verify: correct HTTP status code, correct error code from error taxonomy, helpful error message, no sensitive data leaked in error response.

steps.name: boundary_value_tests
steps.instruction: Write tests for boundary and edge cases across all features. Test per data type: strings (empty string, single character, exactly at max length, one over max length, Unicode emoji, RTL characters, CJK characters, zero-width characters, HTML entities, very long single word with no spaces), numbers (0, -1, MAX_INT, MIN_INT, decimal precision limits, NaN, Infinity), dates (epoch, far future, far past, timezone edge cases, daylight saving transition), arrays/lists (empty array, single item, at pagination limit, one over limit), files (0 byte file, exactly at size limit, one byte over limit, file with no extension, file with double extension, file with uppercase extension). For each test: verify the system either handles it gracefully or returns a clear validation error.

steps.name: concurrency_tests
steps.instruction: Write tests for concurrent access scenarios. Test: double_submit (submit the same form twice rapidly — should not create duplicates, test with idempotency key if implemented), concurrent_edit (two users edit the same resource simultaneously — should handle with optimistic locking or last-write-wins with clear behavior), race_condition_signup (same email registers twice simultaneously — only one should succeed, other gets conflict), concurrent_delete_and_edit (one user deletes while another edits — should handle gracefully), bulk_operations_under_load (if bulk endpoints exist: submit large batch while other operations are running). Use Promise.all or concurrent test runners to simulate simultaneous requests. Verify: no data corruption, no duplicate records, appropriate error responses for losing requests.

steps.name: data_integrity_tests
steps.instruction: Write tests verifying data integrity across the system. Test: cascade_deletes (delete a parent entity, verify all children are deleted or handled per schema ON DELETE behavior), referential_integrity (attempt to create a record referencing non-existent foreign key — should fail), unique_constraints (attempt to violate each unique constraint — should fail with clear error), transaction_rollback (if a multi-step operation fails midway, verify all changes are rolled back — no partial state), data_consistency_after_errors (after various error scenarios, verify database is in a consistent state), soft_delete_behavior (if using soft delete: deleted records don't appear in queries, can't be edited, unique constraints handle soft-deleted records correctly).

steps.name: e2e_suite_review
steps.instruction: Review the complete E2E test suite for coverage completeness. Verify these critical journeys are covered end-to-end: new_user_signup (landing → signup → verify email → onboard → first value action), returning_user (login → dashboard → core action → logout), billing_journey (signup → use free tier → hit limit → upgrade → payment → access expanded features), account_management (login → change password → update profile → change email → verify new email), error_recovery (encounter error → see helpful message → retry or navigate away → continue using app), admin_journey (if admin features: login as admin → manage users → moderate content). Flag any critical journey without an E2E test.

steps.name: missing_e2e_tests
steps.condition: Only execute if e2e_review_result identifies missing journeys
steps.instruction: Write E2E tests for any critical user journeys flagged as missing in e2e_review_result. Each test should: use realistic data (not 'test123'), run against a seeded database with known state, complete in under 60 seconds, clean up after itself, use accessible selectors (data-testid, role, label text — not CSS classes or XPath).

steps.name: performance_test_creation
steps.instruction: Create load testing scripts using k6, Artillery, or equivalent. Script scenarios: baseline_load (expected normal traffic: X requests per minute distributed across endpoints weighted by expected usage pattern), peak_load (2-3x baseline: simulating marketing campaign or viral moment), stress_test (ramp from 0 to breaking point: increase by 10% every minute until error rate exceeds 5%), spike_test (instant jump to 10x baseline, hold for 5 minutes, drop back to baseline), endurance_test (baseline load sustained for 1 hour: checks for memory leaks, connection pool exhaustion). For each scenario: define virtual user behavior (login, navigate, perform actions, think time between actions), define assertions (p95 latency < target, error rate < 1%), define metrics to capture (latency percentiles, throughput, error rate, resource utilization).

steps.name: baseline_performance_test
steps.instruction: Run the baseline_load performance test against staging. Record results: requests_per_second, latency_p50, latency_p95, latency_p99, error_rate, errors_by_type. Also monitor during test: server CPU utilization, memory usage, database connections, database query time, cache hit rate. Identify any endpoints that exceed NFR latency targets even at baseline load. Output: baseline_results (all metrics), slow_endpoints (list of endpoints exceeding targets with observed latency).

steps.name: stress_test
steps.instruction: Run the stress_test scenario against staging. Gradually increase load until the system breaks. Record: breaking_point (requests per minute when error rate exceeded 5%), failure_mode (what broke first: CPU, memory, database connections, timeout, OOM), max_concurrent_users_before_degradation, max_throughput_before_degradation. Compare breaking point to nfr_scalability targets. If breaking point is below target: flag as critical issue.

steps.name: performance_analysis
steps.instruction: Analyze all performance test results. Identify: bottleneck_endpoints (slowest endpoints with root cause hypothesis: slow query, missing index, missing cache, heavy computation), resource_bottlenecks (which resource hits limit first: CPU, memory, DB connections, disk I/O), scaling_recommendations (what to optimize vs when to scale horizontally), cost_of_scaling (estimated infrastructure cost to handle 2x, 5x, 10x current capacity). Prioritize optimizations: highest impact first, cheapest first for equal impact. Output: ranked optimization list with expected improvement per optimization.

steps.name: performance_optimization
steps.instruction: Implement the top-priority performance optimizations from performance_analysis. Common fixes: add missing database indexes (use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to verify improvement), implement caching for slow read endpoints (follow caching_strategy), fix N+1 queries (use eager loading or dataloader pattern), optimize large payloads (add field selection, pagination), add database connection pooling tuning, optimize frontend bundle (code split, tree shake, lazy load), add compression for large responses. After each optimization: re-run the relevant performance test to measure improvement. Document: optimization_applied, before_metric, after_metric, improvement_percentage.

steps.name: post_optimization_verification
steps.instruction: Re-run baseline and stress tests after optimizations. Compare results to pre-optimization baselines. Verify: all endpoints now meet NFR latency targets at baseline load, breaking point improved from stress test, no regressions (endpoints that were fast didn't get slower). Output: comparison_table (metric, before, after, change_percent).

steps.name: cross_browser_testing
steps.instruction: Test the application on all browsers from platform_requirements. For each browser: load every page, verify layout renders correctly, test core user flow (signup → core action), test JavaScript functionality (forms, modals, dropdowns, infinite scroll), check console for errors. Browsers to test: Chrome (latest), Firefox (latest), Safari (latest), Edge (latest). If mobile: Chrome Mobile, Safari iOS. Document: browser, version, issues_found (with screenshots if possible). Common issues: CSS flex/grid differences, Safari date input, Firefox focus styles, Safari 100vh bug.

steps.name: mobile_device_testing
steps.instruction: Test on mobile viewports and touch interactions. Test at breakpoints: 375px (iPhone SE), 390px (iPhone 14), 412px (Android common), 768px (iPad). Verify: all content is visible without horizontal scroll, touch targets are minimum 44x44px, forms work with virtual keyboard (inputs not hidden behind keyboard), pull-to-refresh works if implemented, swipe gestures work if implemented, orientation change (portrait → landscape) doesn't break layout, bottom navigation doesn't overlap with phone's home indicator. Document: viewport, issues_found.

steps.name: responsive_visual_check
steps.instruction: Review screenshots or visual descriptions from cross-browser and mobile testing. Verify: layouts match responsive_spec at each breakpoint, no visual regressions between browsers, typography is readable at all sizes, colors render consistently, interactive elements are distinguishable (not hidden or overlapping), no content overflow. Flag issues with: page, breakpoint, description, severity.

steps.name: cross_platform_fixes
steps.condition: Only execute if issues were found
steps.instruction: Fix all cross-browser and responsive issues found in testing. Common fixes: add vendor prefixes, use CSS feature queries (@supports), add polyfills for missing APIs, use safe area insets for notched devices, fix viewport unit issues (use dvh instead of vh), add touch-action CSS for gesture handling. Re-verify each fix on the affected browser/device.

steps.name: automated_a11y_scan
steps.instruction: Run automated accessibility testing on every page. Tools: axe-core (via browser extension or Playwright integration), Lighthouse accessibility audit. For each page: run scan, collect all violations. Categorize violations by WCAG level (A, AA, AAA) and impact (critical, serious, moderate, minor). Output: violations_by_page (page, violation_count, critical_count), all_violations (list with: page, element, rule, impact, WCAG criterion, remediation suggestion). Zero critical and serious violations is the target.

steps.name: keyboard_navigation_test
steps.instruction: Test every page and flow using only keyboard. For each page: start from the browser address bar, Tab through all interactive elements, verify: focus is visible at all times (no invisible focus), focus order is logical (follows visual layout, not random DOM order), all buttons and links are reachable, all form fields can be filled, dropdown menus open with Enter/Space and navigate with arrow keys, modals trap focus, modals close with Escape, skip link works (if present), no focus traps (can always Tab out). Document: page, issues (element, expected behavior, actual behavior).

steps.name: screen_reader_test
steps.instruction: Test key flows with screen reader simulation. Verify for each page: page title is announced on navigation, headings form a logical hierarchy (h1 → h2 → h3), all images have appropriate alt text, form fields announce their labels, error messages are announced when they appear (via aria-live), buttons announce their purpose, dynamic content changes are announced, tables have proper header associations, custom components announce their role and state. Document: page, issue, ARIA fix needed.

steps.name: color_contrast_check
steps.instruction: Verify all text/background color combinations meet WCAG 2.1 AA contrast requirements. Check: normal text (< 18px or < 14px bold) requires 4.5:1 ratio, large text (>= 18px or >= 14px bold) requires 3:1 ratio, UI components and graphical objects require 3:1 ratio against adjacent colors. Check all color combinations used in the design system: text on backgrounds, buttons, links, badges, alerts, form elements. Use design_tokens to calculate ratios programmatically. Flag any failing combinations with: foreground_color, background_color, current_ratio, required_ratio, where_used.

steps.name: a11y_fixes
steps.instruction: Fix all accessibility violations found across automated scans, keyboard testing, screen reader testing, and contrast checking. Prioritize: critical and serious violations first, then moderate, then minor. Common fixes: add missing alt text, add aria-label to icon buttons, fix heading hierarchy, add aria-live to dynamic regions, fix focus management in modals and dropdowns, add visible focus indicators, fix color contrast (update design tokens if needed — but verify change doesn't break visual design). Re-run automated scan after fixes to verify.

steps.name: full_regression_suite
steps.instruction: Run the complete test suite after all testing phase fixes: unit tests, integration tests, E2E tests, accessibility scan. All must pass. If any failures: route to fixer agent. This is the final quality gate before security hardening. Output: total_tests, passed, failed, coverage_percentage, a11y_violations_remaining.

steps.name: bug_compilation
steps.instruction: Compile all bugs discovered during the entire testing phase. For each bug: bug_id, title, description, severity (critical: blocks launch or data loss, high: major feature broken, medium: degraded experience with workaround, low: cosmetic or minor), status (fixed, open, deferred), found_in_step (which testing step discovered it), fix_reference (if fixed: which step fixed it). Summary: total_bugs_found, bugs_fixed, bugs_remaining_by_severity. If any critical or high bugs remain unfixed, this is a launch blocker — trigger needs_clarification presenting the bugs and asking human for decision (fix before launch, accept risk, or descope feature).

steps.name: owasp_audit
steps.instruction: Systematically audit the application against each OWASP Top 10 2021 category. For each category: A01_Broken_Access_Control (test: IDOR on every entity type, privilege escalation, CORS misconfiguration, directory traversal, metadata manipulation), A02_Cryptographic_Failures (test: sensitive data in transit without TLS, weak hashing, sensitive data in logs/URLs, missing encryption at rest), A03_Injection (test: SQL injection on all inputs, NoSQL injection, command injection, LDAP injection, XSS stored/reflected/DOM), A04_Insecure_Design (review: business logic flaws, missing rate limiting, missing fraud prevention), A05_Security_Misconfiguration (test: default credentials, unnecessary features enabled, verbose errors, missing security headers, outdated software), A06_Vulnerable_Components (test: known CVEs in dependencies), A07_Auth_Failures (test: credential stuffing, brute force, session fixation, weak passwords allowed), A08_Data_Integrity (test: deserialization attacks, unsigned updates), A09_Logging_Failures (test: sensitive actions not logged, logs missing user context), A10_SSRF (test: user-supplied URLs, webhook URLs, import URLs). For each finding: owasp_category, description, severity, reproduction_steps, remediation.

steps.name: injection_testing
steps.instruction: Deep-dive injection testing on all user inputs. For every input field and API parameter: test SQL injection payloads (OR 1=1, UNION SELECT, stacked queries, blind injection with time delay), test NoSQL injection (if MongoDB: $gt, $ne operators in JSON), test command injection (if any system calls: pipe characters, semicolons, backticks), test header injection (CRLF in headers), test path traversal (../../etc/passwd in file parameters). Verify: all queries use parameterized statements or ORM (never string concatenation), all system calls use safe APIs (no shell: true), all file paths are validated against allowed directories. Output: input_tested, payload_used, result (blocked/vulnerable), evidence.

steps.name: xss_testing
steps.instruction: Test for Cross-Site Scripting on every output that reflects user input. Test: stored_XSS (submit <script>alert(1)</script> in every text field, then view the page that displays it — verify it's encoded), reflected_XSS (inject script in URL parameters, verify output encoding), DOM_XSS (inject in fragment/hash, test client-side rendering of URL parameters). Test with various payloads: basic script tag, img onerror, svg onload, event handlers, javascript: URIs, data: URIs, template literals. Verify: Content-Security-Policy header is set and blocks inline scripts, output encoding is applied everywhere (HTML entities, not just sanitization), React/framework's built-in XSS protection is not bypassed (no dangerouslySetInnerHTML with user input).

steps.name: csrf_testing
steps.instruction: Verify CSRF protection on all state-changing endpoints. For each POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE endpoint: attempt the request from a different origin (create a test HTML page on a different domain that submits a form to our API), verify the request is rejected. Check: if using JWT in Authorization header (not cookies) — CSRF is inherently mitigated. If using cookies: verify SameSite attribute is set (Strict or Lax), verify CSRF token is required and validated, verify token is unique per session. Test: replaying a valid CSRF token from a different session (should fail), omitting the token (should fail), using an expired token (should fail).

steps.name: auth_security_review
steps.instruction: Deep-dive authentication security review. Test: password_hashing (verify bcrypt/argon2 is used with appropriate work factor — extract a hash from DB and verify format), brute_force_protection (attempt 20 rapid login failures — verify account lockout or progressive delay activates), session_management (verify access token expiry is enforced — use an expired token and verify 401, verify refresh token rotation — used token cannot be reused, verify refresh token revocation on password change), token_storage (verify tokens are not in localStorage if using cookies — check httpOnly, Secure, SameSite attributes), password_policy (verify minimum length, complexity requirements from business_rules are enforced), account_enumeration (verify login error message is the same for 'wrong email' and 'wrong password' — no information leakage), registration_abuse (verify rate limiting on registration endpoint, verify email verification is required before access).

steps.name: authorization_testing
steps.instruction: Comprehensive authorization testing. For every API endpoint that accesses a resource: test IDOR (authenticate as User A, request User B's resource by ID — must return 403 or 404, never the data), test privilege_escalation (authenticate as regular user, attempt admin-only endpoints — must return 403), test horizontal_access (User A attempts to edit/delete User B's resources), test role_downgrade (if a user's role is changed, verify their existing sessions reflect the new role immediately or on next token refresh), test deleted_user (delete a user, verify their tokens no longer work), test API_parameter_tampering (send user_id in request body trying to impersonate another user — should be ignored, use session user instead). Output: endpoint, test_type, expected_result, actual_result, pass/fail.

steps.name: api_security_review
steps.instruction: API-specific security review. Test: rate_limiting (verify every endpoint has a rate limit, verify the limit is enforced, verify 429 response includes Retry-After header), input_validation (verify every parameter is validated — send unexpected fields, oversized payloads, deeply nested JSON, circular references), cors_configuration (verify only allowed origins can make requests, verify credentials mode is correctly configured, verify no wildcard * with credentials), mass_assignment (send extra fields in create/update requests that shouldn't be settable — role, is_admin, created_at — verify they're ignored), verbose_errors (in production mode, verify 500 errors don't include stack traces, SQL queries, or internal paths), http_methods (verify only documented methods are allowed per endpoint — OPTIONS should be limited, HEAD should not expose data), response_headers (verify no sensitive headers leak server version, technology stack).

steps.name: dependency_vulnerability_scan
steps.instruction: Run comprehensive dependency vulnerability scan. Execute: npm audit (or pip audit, cargo audit, etc.), run Snyk or equivalent if available, check GitHub Dependabot alerts if enabled. For each vulnerability found: package_name, vulnerability_id (CVE), severity (critical/high/medium/low), description, affected_version, fixed_version (if available), exploitability (is this actually exploitable in our usage?), path (how we depend on it: direct or transitive). Output: total_vulnerabilities_by_severity, actionable_list (vulnerabilities we can fix by updating).

steps.name: dependency_vulnerability_fixes
steps.instruction: Fix all critical and high severity dependency vulnerabilities from dependency_scan_result. For each: if fix_available (newer version without vulnerability): update the dependency, run full test suite to verify no breakage. If no fix available: evaluate if we can replace the dependency with an alternative, evaluate if we can mitigate the vulnerability in our code, if neither: document as accepted risk with justification. For medium vulnerabilities: update if straightforward (patch/minor version bump), defer if major version bump required (schedule for next iteration). Output: fixed_count, deferred_count, accepted_risk_count with justifications.

steps.name: security_headers_audit
steps.instruction: Audit and verify all security headers. Required headers: Content-Security-Policy (script-src 'self', style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' if needed, img-src 'self' data: and CDN domain, connect-src 'self' and API domain, frame-ancestors 'none'), X-Frame-Options (DENY), X-Content-Type-Options (nosniff), Strict-Transport-Security (max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains), Referrer-Policy (strict-origin-when-cross-origin), Permissions-Policy (camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=() — restrict unless needed), X-XSS-Protection (0 — deprecated but set to 0 to avoid edge case bugs). For each header: check if present, check if value is correct, test with securityheaders.com or equivalent. Output: headers_present, headers_missing, headers_misconfigured.

steps.name: security_headers_fix
steps.condition: Only execute if headers are missing or misconfigured
steps.instruction: Implement or fix all security headers flagged in security_headers_audit_result. Configure headers in the appropriate layer: application middleware (for API responses), nginx/CDN configuration (for static assets), meta tags (for CSP in HTML if needed). For CSP: start restrictive and add exceptions only as needed, use report-uri or report-to for monitoring violations before enforcing. Test: application still functions correctly with all headers applied (CSP doesn't block legitimate resources, X-Frame-Options doesn't break intended embedding).

steps.name: encryption_verification
steps.instruction: Verify encryption at rest and in transit. Transit: verify TLS 1.2+ on all endpoints (use ssllabs.com for grade), verify no mixed content (HTTP resources on HTTPS page), verify API-to-database connection uses TLS (check connection string), verify API-to-cache connection uses TLS if over network. At rest: verify database encryption is enabled (check provider settings), verify file storage encryption is enabled (S3 default encryption or equivalent), verify backups are encrypted. Key management: verify encryption keys are not in code, verify key rotation is possible, verify separate keys per environment. Output: encryption_status per connection and storage, issues found.

steps.name: secrets_audit
steps.instruction: Audit for leaked secrets. Scan: entire git history (use truffleHog or gitleaks), all source files (grep for patterns: API key formats, password=, secret=, token=, private key headers), all configuration files (verify no .env committed, no hardcoded connection strings), frontend bundle (build the frontend, search the output for any secrets that might be bundled), CI/CD configuration (verify secrets are injected via secure mechanism, not hardcoded in pipeline files), Docker images (inspect layers for accidentally copied .env files). Output: secrets_found (list with: file, line, type, remediation: rotate key + remove from history), status (clean/compromised).

steps.name: logging_security_review
steps.instruction: Review logging for security concerns. Check that logs do NOT contain: passwords (even hashed), authentication tokens (access or refresh), full credit card numbers (last 4 only), Social Security numbers, API keys, PII beyond what's necessary for debugging (full email may be ok, full address is not). Check that logs DO contain: authentication events (login, logout, failed login with IP), authorization failures (who tried to access what), data modification events (who changed what, when), admin actions (all admin operations), security events (rate limit triggers, CSRF failures, suspicious patterns). Verify: log retention meets regulatory requirements, logs are tamper-resistant (append-only, or shipped to external service), log access is restricted.

steps.name: file_upload_security
steps.condition: Only execute if application has file upload functionality
steps.instruction: Review file upload security. Test: file type validation (upload a .php file renamed to .jpg — should be rejected based on magic bytes, not just extension), file size limit enforcement (upload a file exceeding limit — should be rejected before fully uploading), path traversal in filename (upload file named ../../etc/passwd — filename should be sanitized), executable upload (upload .exe, .sh, .bat — should be rejected), double extension (upload file.jpg.php — should be rejected), null byte injection (upload file.jpg%00.php — should be sanitized), virus/malware scanning (if implemented: upload EICAR test file), storage isolation (uploaded files should not be in web-accessible directory, served via signed URLs or through API proxy). Output: test_results per check.

steps.name: security_fixes
steps.instruction: Fix all security vulnerabilities identified across all security testing steps. Prioritize by severity: critical first (active exploitable vulnerabilities), then high (significant risk), then medium. For each fix: describe the vulnerability, the fix applied, and how to verify the fix. Do not change test expectations — fix the application code. After all fixes: re-run the specific security tests that found the vulnerabilities to verify remediation.

steps.name: security_retest
steps.instruction: Re-run all security tests to verify fixes. Execute: OWASP scan, injection tests, XSS tests, auth tests, authorization tests, dependency scan, header check. All previously-found vulnerabilities must be resolved. If any persist: route back to fixer. Output: retest_results, all_clear (boolean).

steps.name: security_audit_report
steps.instruction: Write the Security Audit Report. Sections: 1) Executive Summary (overall security posture, key findings, remediation status), 2) Methodology (what was tested, tools used), 3) Findings (each vulnerability: description, severity, OWASP category, reproduction steps, remediation applied, verification), 4) Accepted Risks (vulnerabilities not fixed with justification and mitigation), 5) Dependency Status (vulnerable dependencies fixed/deferred), 6) Compliance Status (GDPR, OWASP Top 10 coverage), 7) Recommendations (ongoing security practices). Version the report.

steps.name: api_documentation
steps.instruction: Generate complete API documentation from the OpenAPI spec. For each endpoint: description, authentication requirements, request parameters with types and examples, request body with JSON example, response body with JSON example for success and each error case, curl example. Add overview sections: authentication guide (how to obtain and use tokens), pagination guide (how to use cursor/offset), error handling guide (error format, common error codes, retry guidance), rate limiting guide (limits, headers to check, backoff strategy). Publish as interactive documentation (Swagger UI, Redoc, or equivalent).

steps.name: user_getting_started
steps.instruction: Write the Getting Started guide for end users. Structure: 1) Create your account (step by step with description of each screen), 2) Complete setup (onboarding walkthrough), 3) Your first [core action] (guided tutorial of the primary value workflow), 4) Next steps (links to feature-specific guides). Tone: friendly, encouraging, assumes no prior knowledge. Keep each step to 1-2 sentences + description of what they should see. Total guide should be completable in 5 minutes.

steps.name: feature_documentation
steps.instruction: Write per-feature user documentation. For each MVP feature: feature_name, what_it_does (1-2 sentence overview), how_to_access (navigation path), how_to_use (step-by-step for common actions), tips (power user tips, keyboard shortcuts), limitations (known limits: free tier restrictions, max values), troubleshooting (common issues and solutions). Organize by feature area matching the app's navigation structure. Each article should be self-contained (user can find it via search and understand without reading other articles).

steps.name: faq
steps.instruction: Write FAQ from multiple sources: anticipated questions (based on product complexity), competitor review complaints (common confusion points from competitor_reviews_raw), common support patterns (predicted from onboarding flow complexity), billing questions (how billing works, refund policy, plan changes). Structure: categorized (Getting Started, Account, Billing, Features, Troubleshooting), each Q&A: question (natural language, how a user would ask), answer (concise, links to detailed docs where applicable). Include: minimum 20 Q&As.

steps.name: in_app_copy_onboarding
steps.instruction: Write all in-app onboarding copy. For each onboarding screen from user_flow_onboarding: heading, body_text, button_labels, skip_text. For tooltip tour (if implemented): each tooltip: target_element, title, body, step_indicator. For empty states that guide new users: heading, body_text, cta_button_text. Tone: welcoming, action-oriented, concise (under 20 words per body text where possible). Every piece of copy should answer 'what should I do next?'

steps.name: in_app_copy_errors
steps.instruction: Write all user-facing error messages. For each error code in error_handling_strategy taxonomy: user_facing_message (helpful, not technical), suggested_action (what the user can do to resolve it), tone (empathetic, not blaming). Categories: validation errors (per field: 'Email is required', 'Password must be at least 8 characters'), auth errors ('Incorrect email or password', 'Your session has expired, please log in again'), permission errors ('You don't have access to this resource'), not found ('We couldn't find what you're looking for'), rate limit ('You're doing that too fast. Please wait a moment.'), server errors ('Something went wrong on our end. We've been notified and are working on it. Please try again in a few minutes.'). Never expose: stack traces, SQL errors, internal IDs, technical jargon.

steps.name: in_app_copy_notifications
steps.instruction: Write all notification templates. For each email type from email_system_design: subject_line (under 50 chars, action-oriented), preview_text (under 100 chars), body_html_content (heading, body paragraphs, CTA button, footer), body_plain_text_version. For in-app notifications: notification_text (under 100 chars), link_target. Email types to cover: welcome, email_verification, password_reset, payment_receipt, subscription_confirmation, subscription_cancellation, upcoming_renewal, failed_payment, account_deletion_confirmation, feature_announcement. Each template should include: unsubscribe link, company address (CAN-SPAM), support contact.

steps.name: developer_setup_guide
steps.instruction: Update the developer setup guide from Sprint 0 (developer_workflow_doc) with any changes that occurred during implementation. Verify every step still works: clone, install, configure, run, test, build. Add: common gotchas discovered during development, new environment variables added during implementation, any new tools or scripts added. Ensure a new agent joining the project can go from zero to running the full application by following this guide alone.

steps.name: architecture_overview_doc
steps.instruction: Write a developer-facing architecture overview based on the actual built system (may differ from initial technical_design_document). Sections: system diagram (text description of components and connections), tech stack (what we use and why, link to ADRs), project structure (actual directory tree with purpose of each directory), key patterns (authentication flow, data fetching pattern, error handling pattern, testing pattern), database schema overview (tables, key relationships, common queries), API overview (resource structure, conventions), deployment architecture (environments, CI/CD flow, how to deploy). Keep it concise — link to detailed docs rather than duplicating.

steps.name: contributing_guide
steps.instruction: Write CONTRIBUTING.md. Sections: how to report bugs (issue template), how to suggest features (issue template), development setup (link to setup guide), branch naming convention, commit message convention, pull request process (template, review expectations, merge strategy), code style (auto-enforced, just save), testing requirements (what tests are expected for different types of changes), documentation requirements (when to update docs). Keep it welcoming and clear.

steps.name: deployment_runbook
steps.instruction: Write the deployment runbook. Sections: prerequisites (access needed, tools installed), routine_deployment (step-by-step: create release tag, monitor CI/CD, verify health, check metrics), rollback_procedure (step-by-step: identify issue, trigger rollback, verify previous version is live, investigate), database_migration_deployment (extra steps when migrations are involved: backup before, run migration, verify, rollback procedure for failed migration), hotfix_deployment (expedited process: branch from main, fix, test, deploy, backport to develop), environment_variables (how to add/change env vars in each environment), infrastructure_changes (how to scale up, how to add a new service).

steps.name: incident_response_playbook
steps.instruction: Write incident response playbook. Scenarios: complete_outage (detect → assess → communicate → mitigate → resolve → post-mortem), degraded_performance (detect → identify bottleneck → mitigate → optimize → post-mortem), data_breach (detect → contain → assess scope → notify affected users per GDPR timeline → remediate → post-mortem), dependency_outage (detect → activate fallback/degraded mode → monitor dependency status → restore when available), payment_system_down (detect → pause billing-related features → communicate to affected users → restore), database_corruption (detect → stop writes → assess damage → restore from backup → verify integrity). For each: detection method, severity classification, communication templates, step-by-step response, post-mortem template.

steps.name: database_documentation
steps.instruction: Document the database. Sections: schema_overview (entity-relationship description, key tables and their purposes), table_reference (for each table: purpose, columns with descriptions, relationships, common queries), migration_history (list of migrations with description of what each changed), backup_and_restore (how backups work, how to restore, tested procedure), connection_management (connection pool settings, how to monitor connections), common_operations (how to add a column, how to add an index, how to run a one-off data migration), performance_notes (known slow queries and their mitigation, indexes and their purpose).

steps.name: env_var_documentation
steps.instruction: Document every environment variable. For each: variable_name, description (what it's for), required (yes/no), default_value (if any), valid_values (format, range, enum options), where_to_obtain (for third-party keys: link to provider dashboard), environment_specific_notes (different in development vs production?), security_classification (secret: never log or expose, config: safe to log). Generate from the actual .env.example file to ensure nothing is missed. Group by category: application, database, cache, auth, email, storage, payment, analytics, monitoring.

steps.name: readme
steps.instruction: Write README.md. Sections: project_name and tagline, badges (CI status, coverage, license), one_paragraph_description, key_features (bullet list), tech_stack (with version badges), quick_start (5 steps max to run locally — link to full setup guide for details), project_structure (abbreviated directory tree), links (to detailed docs: API docs, architecture overview, contributing guide, deployment runbook), license. Keep README under 200 lines. It should give a clear picture of what this project is and how to get started in under 2 minutes of reading.

steps.name: changelog
steps.instruction: Create CHANGELOG.md following Keep a Changelog format. Create the v1.0.0 entry with sections: Added (every feature in the MVP, grouped by epic), Security (security measures implemented), Infrastructure (deployment, monitoring, CI/CD). Future entries will follow the same format with: Added, Changed, Deprecated, Removed, Fixed, Security. Date format: YYYY-MM-DD.

steps.name: documentation_review
steps.instruction: Review all documentation for: accuracy (do instructions actually work?), completeness (any undocumented features or config?), consistency (same terminology everywhere?), readability (clear language, appropriate detail level for audience?), links (no broken links between docs?), outdated info (does anything reference pre-implementation plans instead of actual implementation?). Output: issues_by_document, overall_quality_score, priority_fixes.

steps.name: documentation_fixes
steps.condition: Only execute if docs_review_result has issues
steps.instruction: Fix all documentation issues from docs_review_result. Prioritize: accuracy errors first (wrong instructions that would mislead), then completeness gaps (missing docs), then readability improvements. For each fix: update the source document, verify links still work, ensure terminology is consistent with the rest of the documentation. Do not rewrite sections that were not flagged — minimize changes to reviewed content.

steps.name: terms_of_service
steps.instruction: Draft Terms of Service. Sections: acceptance_of_terms (by using the service you agree), description_of_service (what we provide), user_accounts (registration requirements, account security responsibility, one person per account), acceptable_use (prohibited activities: abuse, illegal content, reverse engineering, scraping, impersonation), user_content (user retains ownership, grants us license to host/display, user responsible for their content), intellectual_property (our IP: branding, code, design — all rights reserved), payment_terms (if applicable: billing cycle, auto-renewal, price changes with notice, refund policy), termination (we can terminate for violation, user can terminate anytime, what happens to data on termination), limitation_of_liability (service provided as-is, no warranty of uptime, liability capped at fees paid), indemnification (user indemnifies us for their content/actions), dispute_resolution (governing law, jurisdiction, arbitration clause if applicable), modifications (we can update terms with notice, continued use constitutes acceptance), contact_information. Use clear language, not dense legalese. Add prominent note that this is AI-generated and recommend professional legal review before launch.

steps.name: privacy_policy
steps.instruction: Draft Privacy Policy based on actual data practices in the application. Sections: information_we_collect (enumerate exactly: account data (email, name, password hash), usage data (page views, feature usage, IP address, browser), payment data (handled by Stripe — we don't store card numbers), cookies (list each cookie from cookie_policy step)), how_we_use_information (service delivery, improvement, communication, security, legal compliance — be specific per data type), information_sharing (third parties we share with: payment processor, email service, analytics, hosting — name each with purpose), data_retention (how long we keep each data type, deletion timeline after account closure), user_rights (access: request copy of your data, correction: update inaccurate data, deletion: request account and data deletion, portability: export in machine-readable format, objection: opt out of certain processing), security_measures (encryption, access controls, monitoring — general not specific), international_transfers (if data crosses borders: mechanisms used), children (not intended for under 13/16, COPPA/GDPR-K compliance), changes_to_policy (notification method, effective date), contact (data protection contact, how to exercise rights). Add note recommending professional legal review.

steps.name: cookie_policy
steps.instruction: Draft Cookie Policy. List every cookie and tracking mechanism the application uses. For each: cookie_name, provider (first-party or third-party name), purpose (strictly_necessary, functionality, analytics, marketing), duration (session or persistent with expiry), data_stored (what information the cookie contains). Categories: strictly_necessary (session cookie, CSRF token, auth token — cannot be disabled), functional (language preference, theme preference, dismissed banners), analytics (analytics service cookies — e.g., PostHog, Mixpanel), marketing (none for MVP typically). Include: what cookies are, how users can manage cookies (browser settings, our consent tool), impact of disabling cookies (what stops working). Keep concise and factual.

steps.name: cookie_consent_implementation
steps.instruction: Implement cookie consent mechanism per cookie_policy. Create: cookie consent banner (shown on first visit: brief explanation, accept all / customize / reject non-essential buttons), preference center (accessible from footer: toggle each cookie category, save preferences), consent_storage (store user's consent choice in a strictly-necessary cookie), enforcement (before consent: only set strictly_necessary cookies, block analytics and marketing scripts, after consent: set cookies per user's choices, inject/block analytics scripts accordingly), consent_record (log: user_id or anonymous_id, timestamp, choices made — for GDPR compliance). Implement: re-consent trigger (if cookie policy changes, show banner again), consent_api (for checking consent status in code before setting cookies or loading scripts). Accessible: banner is keyboard navigable, screen reader announces it.

steps.name: gdpr_compliance_check
steps.instruction: Verify GDPR compliance if serving EU users. Check: lawful_basis (is there a legal basis for each data processing activity? consent, contract, legitimate interest), consent_management (consent is freely given, specific, informed, unambiguous — opt-in not pre-checked, easy to withdraw), right_to_access (user can request all data we hold about them — is this implemented? data export feature), right_to_rectification (user can correct their data — edit profile, contact support), right_to_erasure (user can delete their account and all data — is account deletion implemented? cascade to all user data? within 30 days), right_to_portability (user can export data in machine-readable format — JSON/CSV export), data_processing_records (do we maintain records of processing activities?), dpa_with_processors (Data Processing Agreements with all third-party services that process personal data: hosting, analytics, email, payment), breach_notification_process (can we detect and notify within 72 hours?), privacy_by_design (is data minimization practiced? do we collect only what's needed?). Output: compliant_items, non_compliant_items with remediation.

steps.name: ccpa_compliance_check
steps.instruction: Verify CCPA compliance if serving California users. Check: right_to_know (users can request what personal information is collected, used, shared — privacy policy covers this), right_to_delete (users can request deletion — account deletion implemented), right_to_opt_out (if we sell data: 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link — typically we don't sell data, but verify no third-party sharing constitutes 'sale' under CCPA), non_discrimination (users exercising rights receive same service and pricing), notice_at_collection (privacy policy describes collection at or before point of collection), financial_incentives (if offering discounts for data: disclose terms). If CCPA doesn't apply (under revenue/data thresholds): document why and revisit at scale. Output: applicable (yes/no with reasoning), compliant_items, gaps.

steps.name: license_audit
steps.instruction: Audit all open-source dependencies for license compliance. For each dependency (direct and transitive): package_name, license_type (MIT, Apache-2.0, BSD, GPL, LGPL, ISC, etc.), license_compatibility (compatible with our product license?). Flag: GPL/AGPL dependencies (copyleft — may require our code to be open-source if linked), SSPL dependencies (Server Side Public License — restrictions on SaaS use), unlicensed dependencies (no license specified — legally risky), custom/proprietary licenses (need individual review). Check: attribution requirements (MIT/BSD require copyright notice preservation — verify we include NOTICE/LICENSE files or attribution page), patent clauses (Apache 2.0 grants patent license — be aware). Output: total_dependencies, license_distribution (count per license type), flagged_dependencies (list with issue and recommendation: replace, accept risk, add attribution).

steps.name: product_license_selection
steps.instruction: Select the license for the product itself. Options: proprietary (all rights reserved — standard for commercial SaaS), open_source with permissive license (MIT, Apache 2.0 — allows anyone to use/modify/distribute), open_source with copyleft (GPL, AGPL — requires derivative works to be open source), source_available (BSL, SSPL, Elastic License — viewable source but restricted use). Present options with trade-offs: proprietary (maximum control, no community contribution), open source (community contribution, trust/transparency, but competitors can fork). This is a significant business decision. Trigger needs_clarification presenting options and asking human to decide. Add LICENSE file to repository based on decision.

steps.name: legal_review
steps.instruction: Final review of all legal and compliance documents. Check: internal_consistency (ToS and Privacy Policy don't contradict each other), completeness (all required sections present per jurisdiction), accuracy (data practices described match actual implementation), readability (clear enough for non-lawyers to understand), links (privacy policy links to cookie policy, ToS references privacy policy). Flag: any areas where professional legal review is strongly recommended (data breach procedures, liability limitations, dispute resolution, international compliance). Trigger needs_clarification: present a summary of legal readiness, flag areas needing professional review, recommend specific areas where paid legal counsel would reduce risk significantly. Output: legal_readiness_score, professional_review_recommendations.

steps.name: production_compute
steps.instruction: Provision production compute resources on the chosen hosting platform. Configure: instance type/size (based on nfr_scalability baseline targets + 50% headroom), auto-scaling group (min: 2 instances for redundancy, max: based on scalability_plan), health check (point to /health endpoint, check interval 30s, unhealthy threshold 3 consecutive failures), load balancer (distribute traffic across instances, TLS termination, connection draining on instance shutdown, sticky sessions if required by realtime_design). Verify: instances start and pass health check, load balancer distributes traffic, auto-scaling triggers correctly on simulated load. Output: compute_config, instance_ids, load_balancer_url.

steps.name: production_database
steps.instruction: Provision production database. Configure: instance_type (based on nfr_scalability data volume estimates + headroom), storage (auto-scaling, encrypted), automated_backups (daily snapshots, retain 30 days, continuous WAL archiving for point-in-time recovery), connection_limits (based on max compute instances × connection pool size per instance), network (private subnet, accessible only from compute instances, not publicly accessible), read_replica (if nfr_scalability requires: provision one read replica in same region), monitoring (enable performance insights / slow query logging). Verify: compute instances can connect, query execution works, backup runs successfully. Output: database_endpoint, connection_verified, backup_configured.

steps.name: production_cache
steps.instruction: Provision production cache (Redis). Configure: instance_type (based on expected cache size from caching_strategy), persistence (RDB snapshots if cache data should survive restarts, or no persistence if cache is purely ephemeral), eviction_policy (allkeys-lru recommended), maxmemory (set to 75% of instance memory to avoid OOM), network (private subnet, same as compute), monitoring (memory usage, hit rate, connection count). If high availability needed: enable Redis cluster or replication with automatic failover. Verify: compute instances can connect, set/get operations work. Output: cache_endpoint, connection_verified.

steps.name: dns_configuration
steps.instruction: Configure DNS for production. Create records: A/CNAME for root domain (yourdomain.com) pointing to load balancer or CDN, A/CNAME for www subdomain (redirect to root or vice versa), A/CNAME for api subdomain (api.yourdomain.com) pointing to API load balancer if separate, A/CNAME for app subdomain if SPA is separate from marketing site, MX records for email (if using custom domain email), TXT records for email verification (SPF, DKIM, DMARC from email_system_design). Verify: DNS resolves correctly from multiple locations, propagation complete, no conflicting records. Output: dns_records_created, propagation_verified.

steps.name: ssl_tls_setup
steps.instruction: Configure SSL/TLS for production. Provision certificates: Let's Encrypt (auto-renewal) or AWS ACM / Cloudflare (managed). Configure: TLS 1.2 minimum (disable TLS 1.0, 1.1), strong cipher suites, HSTS header (max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload), HTTP to HTTPS redirect (301 permanent redirect), certificate for all subdomains (wildcard or individual). Test: SSL Labs scan (target A+ grade), verify no mixed content warnings, verify certificate chain is complete, verify auto-renewal is configured. Output: ssl_grade, certificate_expiry, auto_renewal_configured.

steps.name: cdn_configuration
steps.instruction: Configure CDN for production. Configure: origins (API origin, frontend origin, storage origin for uploaded files), caching_rules (static assets with content hash: Cache-Control max-age=31536000 immutable, HTML/index files: Cache-Control no-cache must-revalidate, API responses: no CDN caching — pass through, uploaded files: Cache-Control max-age=86400), compression (gzip and brotli for text assets), custom_domain (use production domain), edge_locations (based on target audience geography), security (WAF rules if available, DDoS protection, bot protection). Verify: assets load from CDN edge, cache hit rate is high for static assets, API requests pass through without caching. Output: cdn_url, cache_hit_rate_initial.

steps.name: email_delivery_setup
steps.instruction: Configure production email delivery. Set up: domain_authentication (SPF record: include sending service, DKIM: add key records, DMARC: p=none initially to monitor, then p=quarantine after verification), sender_configuration (from: noreply@yourdomain.com for transactional, support@yourdomain.com for replies), bounce_handling (configure bounce webhook, handle hard bounces: mark email invalid, soft bounces: retry 3 times), complaint_handling (configure feedback loop, auto-unsubscribe on complaint), sending_limits (verify production sending limits are sufficient for expected volume). Test: send test emails from production environment, verify delivery to Gmail, Outlook, Apple Mail, Yahoo, check spam folder, run mail-tester.com or equivalent (target score 9+/10). Output: deliverability_score, authentication_records_verified.

steps.name: waf_ddos_setup
steps.instruction: Configure web application firewall and DDoS protection. WAF rules: block known bad user agents (scanners, scrapers), block requests from high-risk IP ranges (optional), rate limit by IP (complement application-level rate limiting), block common attack patterns (SQL injection signatures, XSS payloads in URL), custom rules for application-specific patterns. DDoS: enable layer 3/4 protection (TCP flood, UDP flood), enable layer 7 protection (HTTP flood), configure challenge pages for suspicious traffic. Configure: alert on WAF blocks exceeding threshold, whitelist monitoring and health check IPs. Verify: legitimate traffic is not blocked, WAF blocks test attack patterns. Output: waf_rules_active, ddos_protection_level.

steps.name: rate_limiting_production
steps.instruction: Configure production rate limiting. Apply at two layers: infrastructure_level (CDN/load balancer: global rate limit per IP, e.g., 1000 requests per minute), application_level (per-endpoint limits from api_endpoints specs: auth endpoints tighter like 10/minute, read endpoints looser like 100/minute, write endpoints moderate like 30/minute). Configure: response on rate limit (429 status, Retry-After header with seconds until reset, JSON error body matching error format), rate limit storage (Redis from production_cache), rate limit headers on all responses (X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset). Verify: limits are enforced, recovery after limit resets, legitimate burst traffic is not unfairly blocked.

steps.name: autoscaling_configuration
steps.instruction: Configure auto-scaling for production per scalability_plan. Configure: scaling_metric (CPU utilization recommended: scale out at 70%, scale in at 30%), min_instances (2 for redundancy), max_instances (from scalability_plan), scale_out_cooldown (120 seconds — don't scale too fast), scale_in_cooldown (300 seconds — don't scale down too fast), scaling_step (add/remove 1 instance at a time for gradual scaling). If using container orchestration: configure horizontal pod autoscaler with same parameters. Verify: simulate high CPU load, verify new instance launches within 3 minutes, verify traffic is routed to new instance, remove load and verify scale-in after cooldown. Output: autoscaling_config, scale_out_test_result, scale_in_test_result.

steps.name: apm_setup
steps.instruction: Deploy Application Performance Monitoring to production. Install APM agent in application code (Datadog, New Relic, or open-source: OpenTelemetry → Grafana). Configure: transaction_tracing (trace every request through all layers: load balancer → app → database → cache → external APIs), error_capture (capture all unhandled exceptions with context), custom_metrics (from monitoring_strategy: signup_rate, active_users, queue_depth), dependency_mapping (auto-discover connections between services), source_map_upload (for frontend error stack traces). Verify: traces appear in dashboard, errors are captured with stack traces, response time metrics are accurate. Output: apm_dashboard_url, initial_metrics.

steps.name: infrastructure_monitoring
steps.instruction: Set up infrastructure monitoring. For each component (compute, database, cache, load balancer): install monitoring agent or enable cloud provider monitoring, configure metrics collection (CPU, memory, disk, network, component-specific: DB connections, cache hit rate, LB request count). Create dashboards per monitoring_strategy: system_overview (all components on one page with traffic light status), compute_detail (per-instance CPU, memory, network, request count), database_detail (connections, query time, replication lag, disk usage), cache_detail (memory usage, hit/miss rate, connections, evictions). Verify: metrics are flowing, dashboards show real data. Output: dashboard_urls.

steps.name: error_tracking_setup
steps.instruction: Deploy error tracking to production. Install Sentry (or equivalent) in both backend and frontend. Configure: backend (capture unhandled exceptions, attach request context: URL, method, user_id, request_id), frontend (capture unhandled JS errors, capture unhandled promise rejections, upload source maps for readable stack traces per release), release_tracking (tag errors with git SHA and version), environment_tagging (production vs staging), alert_rules (new error type → immediate notification, error spike >10x normal rate → immediate notification), ignore_rules (ignore known benign errors: network timeouts from client, browser extension errors), context_enrichment (attach: user_id, user_email, user_plan, feature_flags). Verify: trigger a test error in backend and frontend, verify it appears in error tracking with full context. Output: error_tracking_url, test_errors_captured.

steps.name: log_aggregation_setup
steps.instruction: Set up centralized log aggregation for production. Configure: log_shipping (application logs → aggregation service via agent, sidecar, or stdout capture), log_parsing (parse structured JSON logs into searchable fields: timestamp, level, request_id, user_id, message), log_retention (90 days for production per logging_strategy), log_search (verify: can search by request_id to trace a single request, can search by user_id to see all user activity, can filter by error level, can filter by time range), log_alerting (alert on: error log rate spike, specific error message patterns, authentication failure patterns). Verify: generate application logs, find them in aggregation service within 30 seconds, search by request_id returns correlated logs. Output: log_dashboard_url, search_verified.

steps.name: uptime_monitoring
steps.instruction: Set up external uptime monitoring. Configure: health_check_url (production /health endpoint), check_interval (60 seconds), check_locations (minimum 3 geographic regions matching target audience), alert_on_failure (after 2 consecutive failures from 2+ locations: send alert via configured channel), ssl_monitoring (check certificate expiry, alert at 14 days before expiry), response_time_monitoring (alert if p95 response time exceeds threshold from NFRs). Optionally set up: public status page (status.yourdomain.com) showing current status and incident history. Verify: monitoring is active, test alert by temporarily blocking health check, verify alert arrives. Output: monitoring_url, status_page_url.

steps.name: alert_rules_configuration
steps.instruction: Configure all alert rules per monitoring_strategy. Create alerts: critical_alerts (send immediately via Telegram: complete outage detected by uptime monitor, error rate >5% for 5 minutes, database connection failures, disk usage >90%, memory usage >90% sustained for 10 minutes, security: multiple auth failures from same IP), warning_alerts (send during business hours via Telegram: error rate >1% for 15 minutes, p95 latency >2x target for 10 minutes, disk usage >75%, cache hit rate <50%, deployment failure, SSL certificate expiry <14 days), info_alerts (log to dashboard only: new error type first occurrence, dependency latency increase, scaling event triggered). For each alert: name, condition, threshold, evaluation_window, notification_channel, runbook_link (link to relevant incident playbook section). Verify: trigger a test alert for each severity level, confirm notification delivery.

steps.name: operational_dashboard
steps.instruction: Create the operational dashboard per monitoring_strategy. Dashboard panels: real_time_section (current error rate, current p95 latency, active users right now, requests per second, server count), health_section (traffic light per component: API green/yellow/red, database, cache, email, storage), performance_section (latency percentiles over time: p50/p95/p99, request volume over time, error rate over time), infrastructure_section (CPU/memory per instance, database connections, cache memory), business_section (signups today, active users today, key feature usage counts).
