Configure: auto-refresh every 30 seconds, time range selector (last hour, last 24h, last 7d), filter by environment. Output: dashboard_url, screenshot_description.

steps.name: analytics_integration
steps.instruction: Integrate analytics SDK in production. Install analytics service (PostHog, Mixpanel, Amplitude, or equivalent). Configure: user_identification (identify user on login with user_id, set user properties: plan, signup_date, role), page_tracking (automatic page view tracking on route change), session_tracking (session duration, pages per session). Respect: cookie_consent (only initialize analytics after user consents to analytics cookies), do_not_track (respect browser DNT header if configured to), ad_blockers (analytics should gracefully degrade if blocked — no errors). Configure for production environment only (no tracking in development/staging unless explicitly enabled). Verify: perform actions in production, verify events appear in analytics dashboard. Output: analytics_configured, test_events_received.

steps.name: event_tracking_implementation
steps.instruction: Implement event tracking for all key actions defined in success_metrics. Track events: signup_started (source, method: email/oauth), signup_completed (method, time_from_start), onboarding_step_completed (step_number, step_name), onboarding_completed (total_time), onboarding_skipped (at_step), first_value_action (action_name, time_from_signup — this is the activation metric), core_feature_used (feature_name, action_type: create/read/update/delete), search_performed (query_length, results_count, filter_used), upgrade_page_viewed (current_plan), upgrade_started (from_plan, to_plan), payment_completed (plan, amount, currency), payment_failed (plan, error_type), subscription_cancelled (plan, reason_if_provided, tenure_days), settings_changed (setting_name), error_encountered (error_code, page). Each event: name, properties (typed object), timestamp (automatic). Verify each event fires correctly.

steps.name: analytics_funnels
steps.instruction: Configure conversion funnels and dashboards in analytics tool. Funnels to create: acquisition_funnel (landing_page_view → signup_started → signup_completed → email_verified), activation_funnel (signup_completed → onboarding_completed → first_value_action), upgrade_funnel (upgrade_page_viewed → upgrade_started → payment_completed), retention_cohort (configure cohort analysis: group users by signup week, measure weekly return rate). Dashboards to create: daily_metrics (DAU, signups, activations, revenue events), weekly_metrics (WAU, retention curve, funnel conversion rates, feature adoption rates), growth_metrics (cumulative users, MRR if applicable, churn rate). Verify: funnels show data from test events, dashboards display correctly. Output: funnel_urls, dashboard_urls.

steps.name: feature_flags_production
steps.instruction: Configure feature flags for production launch. Create flags: maintenance_mode (global kill switch — shows maintenance page), signup_enabled (can disable new signups if overwhelmed), feature_{name} (per core feature — can disable individual features if they cause issues), payment_enabled (can disable payment if payment provider has issues). Configure: default_state (all enabled for launch), gradual_rollout_support (ability to enable a flag for X% of users — useful for future feature releases), admin_override (admin users always see all features regardless of flags). Verify: toggling a flag immediately affects the application, kill switch shows maintenance page. Output: feature_flag_config, kill_switch_tested.

steps.name: production_migration
steps.instruction: Run database migrations on production. Steps: 1) Take a database backup before migration, 2) Run migrations in a maintenance window or during low traffic, 3) Verify migration completed without errors, 4) Verify schema matches expectations (check table count, key columns, indexes), 5) If migration fails: rollback using backup. For production specifically: run migration with a timeout (abort if takes too long), log migration execution time, verify application can connect and query after migration. Output: migration_status, tables_created, execution_time, backup_id.

steps.name: production_seed_data
steps.instruction: Seed production with required operational data only (NOT test data). Required data: admin_account (create the initial admin user — credentials via secure channel, force password change on first login), system_configuration (default settings, feature flag defaults), plan_definitions (if subscription: create the plan records that pricing references), email_templates (if stored in DB: seed production templates), default_categories_or_tags (if the product has predefined categories). Do NOT seed: fake users, sample content, test data. Verify: admin can log in, plans are visible on pricing page, system configuration is applied. Output: seed_status, admin_account_created.

steps.name: staging_final_regression
steps.instruction: Run the complete test suite on staging one final time as the definitive quality gate. Execute: all unit tests, all integration tests, all E2E tests, accessibility scan. This is identical to step 9.24 but run again after all Phase 10-12 changes. All tests must pass. If any fail: route to fixer, fix, and re-run. This is the last automated quality check before launch. Output: total_tests, all_passed, coverage.

steps.name: production_performance_test
steps.instruction: Run performance tests against production infrastructure (with maintenance page still up or feature flags blocking public access). Run the baseline_load scenario from step 9.9. Verify: all endpoints meet NFR performance targets on production hardware, database query times are within targets, cache hit rate is acceptable, no resource bottlenecks under expected load. Compare results to staging performance (production should be equal or better if properly sized). Output: production_performance_results, comparison_to_staging, nfr_compliance (pass/fail per metric).

steps.name: production_security_scan
steps.instruction: Final security scan of production deployment. Check: SSL configuration (grade A+ on SSL Labs), security headers present (all from security_headers_audit), no open ports except 80 and 443, no debug endpoints exposed, no verbose error messages, rate limiting active, WAF active, no default credentials on any service, all environment variables are production values (not staging/test). Run automated scanner (OWASP ZAP or equivalent) against production URL. Output: security_scan_result, issues_found (should be zero — any finding here is a launch blocker).

steps.name: seo_implementation
steps.condition: Only execute if product has public web pages
steps.instruction: Implement SEO fundamentals. For each public page: title tag (unique, under 60 chars, keyword-optimized), meta description (unique, under 160 chars, compelling), canonical URL (self-referencing), Open Graph tags (og:title, og:description, og:image, og:url, og:type), Twitter Card tags (twitter:card, twitter:title, twitter:description, twitter:image). Site-wide: generate sitemap.xml (list all public pages, update frequency, priority), create robots.txt (allow all crawlers on public pages, disallow: /api/, /app/ for authenticated areas, link to sitemap), implement structured data (JSON-LD: Organization for home page, Product/SoftwareApplication if applicable, FAQ if FAQ page exists), verify: semantic HTML (h1 on every page, proper heading hierarchy), accessible images (alt text doubles as SEO), fast load time (Core Web Vitals from Phase 9). Output: seo_checklist_result.

steps.name: social_preview_test
steps.instruction: Test link preview rendering across platforms. Share the production URL on: Twitter/X (use card validator if available), Facebook (use sharing debugger), LinkedIn (use post inspector), Slack (paste URL in a test channel), Discord (paste URL), iMessage/WhatsApp (send to yourself). For each: verify og:title appears correctly, og:description appears correctly, og:image renders (correct dimensions, not cropped badly), URL displays cleanly. Fix any issues in OG tags and re-test. Output: platform, preview_correct (boolean), issues.

steps.name: email_deliverability_test
steps.instruction: Send all email types from production and verify delivery. For each email template: send from production to test accounts on Gmail, Outlook, Apple Mail, Yahoo. Check: arrives in inbox (not spam), renders correctly (images load, links work, responsive on mobile), subject line correct, from address correct, unsubscribe link works, reply-to address works. Run spam score check (mail-tester.com or equivalent: target 9+/10). Verify: SPF passes, DKIM passes, DMARC passes (check email headers). Output: deliverability_results per email type per provider, spam_score.

steps.name: payment_flow_test
steps.condition: Only execute if product has payment functionality
steps.instruction: Test complete payment flow in production. In test mode: create account, navigate to pricing, select paid plan, enter test card (Stripe test card 4242...), complete payment, verify: webhook received, subscription created, access to paid features granted, invoice generated, receipt email sent. Test: upgrade (free → paid), plan change (plan A → plan B), downgrade, cancellation (verify access until period end), failed payment (use declining test card, verify retry logic and email notification), reactivation. After all test mode tests pass: switch to live mode. Verify: Stripe dashboard shows live mode, webhook endpoints updated, API keys rotated. Trigger needs_clarification to confirm human approves going live with payments. Output: all_payment_flows_tested, live_mode_activated.

steps.name: backup_verification
steps.instruction: Verify backup and restore procedure works on production. Steps: 1) Verify automated backup exists (check cloud provider backup list), 2) Restore backup to a temporary database instance (not production), 3) Verify restored data matches production (row counts, recent data present, schema correct), 4) Run a test query on restored database to verify data integrity, 5) Delete temporary restore instance. This validates the disaster_recovery_plan works in practice. Output: backup_exists, restore_successful, data_integrity_verified, restore_time (how long it took — must be within RTO).

steps.name: rollback_verification
steps.instruction: Verify the rollback procedure works. Steps: 1) Deploy current version to production (behind maintenance page), 2) Note the current version/image tag, 3) Deploy a new dummy version (minor change like updating a version string), 4) Verify new version is live, 5) Execute rollback procedure (deploy previous version tag), 6) Verify previous version is restored, 7) Verify no data loss or corruption from the rollback, 8) Verify the whole process completes within acceptable time (under 10 minutes). Document: exact rollback commands, time to rollback, any manual steps required. Output: rollback_tested, rollback_time, procedure_documented.

steps.name: launch_go_no_go
steps.instruction: Compile all pre-launch results into a Go/No-Go assessment. Score each dimension 1-10: functionality (test results: all pass?), performance (meets NFR targets?), security (audit clean?), infrastructure (monitoring, alerting, backups verified?), documentation (complete?), legal (ToS, privacy policy, compliance checks done?), analytics (tracking verified?), operational_readiness (runbooks, incident playbooks, rollback tested?). Calculate: overall_readiness_score (weighted average), launch_blockers (any dimension below 6), launch_risks (any dimension below 8). If blockers exist: list them with remediation needed. If no blockers but risks exist: list risks with mitigation. Trigger needs_clarification presenting the full assessment and asking human for final Go/No-Go decision. Output: readiness_scores, blockers, risks, human_decision.

steps.name: launch_checklist
steps.instruction: Create the final launch checklist. Ordered list of every action to execute during launch, each item: step_number, action (exact command or UI action), verification (how to confirm it worked), rollback (how to undo if something goes wrong), estimated_time (seconds/minutes). Order: 1) Verify all monitoring dashboards are open, 2) Switch payment to live mode (if not already), 3) Enable feature flags for public access, 4) Remove maintenance page / enable public DNS, 5) Verify health check passes, 6) Run post-launch smoke test, 7) Monitor dashboards for 30 minutes, 8) If stable: publish marketing site, 9) Begin launch announcements. Include: pause_points (after step 6: assess stability before proceeding to announcements), abort_criteria (if error rate >1% or health check fails: rollback immediately).

steps.name: launch_checklist_review
steps.instruction: Review launch checklist for: completeness (every necessary action is listed), correct_order (dependencies respected, verification before proceeding), rollback_viability (every step can be undone), timing (total estimated time is reasonable, pause points are in right places), abort_criteria (clear and measurable). Verify: no step depends on a step that comes after it, rollback steps don't have their own dependencies that could fail, the checklist can be executed by the executor agent without ambiguity. Output: checklist_approved (boolean), issues.

steps.name: launch_timing
steps.instruction: Confirm launch timing. Evaluate: day_of_week (Tuesday-Thursday preferred — avoid Monday recovery from weekend and Friday with no support window), time_of_day (morning in primary market timezone — maximum hours for monitoring and response), conflicting_events (major competitor launches, holidays, industry events), team_readiness (all monitoring channels active, human available for decisions). Trigger needs_clarification with recommended launch date/time and ask human to confirm or suggest alternative.

steps.name: execute_launch_payment
steps.condition: Only execute if payment hasn't been switched to live mode yet
steps.instruction: Switch payment integration from test mode to live mode. Execute the exact step from launch_checklist. Verify: Stripe (or equivalent) dashboard shows live mode, webhook endpoint is receiving live events, test a $0 authorization or minimum charge and immediately refund to verify the live connection works. Output: payment_live_mode_active, verification_result.

steps.name: execute_launch_enable
steps.instruction: Enable public access. Execute launch_checklist steps: toggle feature flags to public, remove maintenance page or enable public DNS routing, verify the application is accessible from a public URL without authentication (landing page), verify signup flow works (create a test account from a fresh browser), verify core feature works for the test account, verify health endpoint returns 200. If any verification fails: immediately execute rollback (re-enable maintenance page, disable feature flags) and output failure details. Output: public_access_enabled, smoke_test_results, rollback_needed (boolean).

steps.name: launch_monitoring
steps.instruction: Active monitoring for the first 2 hours after public access is enabled. Check every 5 minutes: error_rate (from error tracking — should be <0.1%), p95_latency (from APM — should be within NFR targets), server_health (CPU <70%, memory <80%, no restarts), database (connections stable, no slow queries), signup_flow (check analytics: are signups coming in? any funnel drop-offs?), error_tracking (any new error types appearing?). Log each check result. If at any point: error_rate >1% for 10 minutes OR health check fails OR server restarts unexpectedly → trigger rollback and alert human. Output: monitoring_log (timestamped checks), stability_assessment (stable/unstable).

steps.name: launch_stability_assessment
steps.instruction: After initial monitoring period, assess launch stability. Compile: error_rate_average, p95_latency_average, total_signups, any_incidents, server_stability_score. If stable (error rate <0.1%, latency within targets, no incidents): recommend proceeding with marketing announcements. If unstable: recommend pausing announcements and fixing issues. If borderline: trigger needs_clarification presenting data and asking human whether to proceed with announcements or wait. Output: stability_status, recommendation, proceed_with_announcements (boolean after human decision if needed).

steps.name: marketing_site_publish
steps.instruction: Publish the marketing site / landing page. Execute: enable public access to marketing site (if separate from app), verify: all pages load, all CTA buttons link to signup, pricing page shows correct plans and prices, legal links (ToS, Privacy Policy) work, all images load, contact/support links work. If marketing site was already live behind the app: verify it's now accessible at the root domain. Output: marketing_site_live, all_links_verified.

steps.name: product_hunt_launch
steps.instruction: Prepare and publish Product Hunt launch. Write: tagline (under 60 characters, compelling), description (under 260 characters, clearly explains what and for whom), first_comment (longer: the story behind the product, the problem, how we solve it differently, what we're looking for from the community, genuine and personal tone), topics (3-5 relevant Product Hunt topics), gallery (describe 3-5 images/screenshots to include: hero shot, key feature screenshot, before/after or workflow demonstration). Note optimal posting time: 12:01 AM PT, Tuesday-Thursday. Output: product_hunt_content_ready, scheduled_time.

steps.name: social_media_launch
steps.instruction: Write and publish launch announcements per platform. Twitter/X: thread (tweet 1: hook/announcement with link, tweet 2: the problem, tweet 3: the solution/key features, tweet 4: what makes us different, tweet 5: CTA — try it free + link). LinkedIn: professional post (problem → solution → key differentiators → CTA, more formal tone). Reddit: genuine post in relevant subreddits (follow each subreddit's rules, be transparent about being the maker, focus on value not promotion, include: what it does, why I built it, link, open to feedback). Hacker News: Show HN post if applicable (title: Show HN: [Product] – [one line description], text: brief explanation + link, genuine tone). Output: posts_by_platform, posted_urls.

steps.name: email_launch
steps.condition: Only execute if waitlist or existing email list exists
steps.instruction: Write and send launch email. Subject line: A/B test two variants (e.g., '[Product] is live!' vs 'The wait is over — [Product] is here'). Body: greeting, announcement (we're live!), what you can do (3 key features with one-line descriptions), how to get started (prominent CTA button: 'Get Started Free'), launch offer (if any: early bird pricing, extended trial), social proof (if any: beta user quote, metric), footer (social links, unsubscribe). Plain text version. Test: verify renders correctly in Gmail, Outlook, Apple Mail before bulk send. Output: email_content, send_result (delivered_count, open_rate after 24h if trackable).

steps.name: app_store_submission
steps.condition: Only execute if product has native mobile apps
steps.instruction: Submit to Apple App Store and/or Google Play Store. Prepare: app_name, subtitle, description (short and full), keywords, category, screenshots (per device type: iPhone 6.5 inch, iPhone 5.5 inch, iPad if applicable, Android phone, Android tablet — each showing key screens with captions), app_icon (all required sizes), privacy_policy_url (live URL), support_url, marketing_url, app_review_notes (login credentials for reviewer, explanation of features). Submit for review. Monitor review status. If rejected: read rejection reason carefully, trigger needs_clarification with rejection details and proposed fix, implement fix, resubmit. Output: submission_status, review_notes_if_rejected.

steps.name: launch_retrospective
steps.instruction: After 24 hours post-launch: compile launch day results. Metrics: total_signups, signup_sources (direct, Product Hunt, social, email), activation_rate (completed onboarding / signups), error_count_total, most_common_errors, average_response_time, peak_concurrent_users, revenue (if applicable), notable_feedback (categorized). Events: incidents (any? severity, resolution time), unexpected_issues, things_that_went_well. Lessons: what_to_repeat, what_to_change, process_improvements. Output: launch_retrospective_report.

steps.name: post_launch_error_monitoring
steps.trigger: Continuous during first week, then triggered by alerts
steps.instruction: Monitor error tracking for new or spiking errors. For each new error: read_stack_trace (identify the failing code path), assess_severity (is it user-facing? does it cause data loss? how many users affected?), determine_root_cause (from stack trace, request context, and logs — correlate via request_id), classify_action (critical: fix immediately via hotfix process, high: fix in next deployment, medium: add to bug backlog, low: add to backlog). For critical issues: diagnose root cause, write minimal fix targeting only the bug, write regression test, deploy hotfix immediately (branch from main, not develop). Output: error_id, severity, root_cause, action_taken.

steps.name: post_launch_performance_monitoring
steps.trigger: Daily during first week, weekly thereafter
steps.instruction: Review performance metrics. Check: p95_latency_trend (increasing, stable, or decreasing — increasing is a concern), error_rate_trend, throughput_trend (are we getting more traffic? is infrastructure handling it?), database_performance (slow query count, connection usage), cache_performance (hit rate, memory usage), resource_utilization (CPU, memory — are we approaching scaling triggers?). Compare against NFR targets. If any metric is trending toward NFR threshold: recommend preemptive optimization or scaling. Output: performance_report, trending_concerns, recommended_actions.

steps.name: feedback_collection_implementation
steps.instruction: Implement in-app user feedback mechanism if not already built during feature implementation. Create: feedback_button (small floating button on bottom-right, labeled 'Feedback' or speech bubble icon), feedback_form (modal on click: category selector (bug, feature request, general feedback), text area for description, optional screenshot/file attachment, optional email for follow-up, submit button), feedback_storage (API endpoint to receive feedback, store in database: user_id, category, message, page_url, browser info, timestamp), feedback_notification (notify team via Telegram when feedback received, include: category, first 100 chars, user email if provided). Also ensure: support email is visible in footer and settings, links to help docs / FAQ are accessible. Output: feedback_mechanism_live.

steps.name: feedback_analysis
steps.instruction: Analyze all user feedback received in the past week. Categorize each piece: bug_report, feature_request, ux_complaint, praise, question, spam. For bugs: deduplicate, check if already known, assess severity. For feature requests: track frequency (how many users asked for the same thing), map to existing backlog items if similar. For UX complaints: identify the specific screen or flow causing confusion. Aggregate: top_5_requested_features (with count), top_5_pain_points (with count), sentiment_trend (more positive or negative than last week?), actionable_insights (specific, concrete things we can do based on feedback). Output: weekly_feedback_analysis.

steps.name: daily_health_report
steps.instruction: Generate a concise daily health report for the human. Include: new_signups_today (count and trend vs yesterday), active_users_today, error_count (and top error if any — name, frequency, severity), average_response_time (p50 and p95, compared to NFR targets), notable_feedback (summarize any standout user feedback received today — bugs reported, feature requests, praise), infrastructure_health (CPU/memory utilization, database connection count, any alerts triggered), revenue_today (if applicable: new subscriptions, cancellations, MRR change), action_items (anything requiring attention — bugs to fix, alerts to investigate, decisions needed). Keep the entire report under 200 words. Format for quick scanning: use short labels and values, not prose. Send via Telegram. If any metric is critically abnormal (error rate >1%, server health degraded, zero signups when expected): flag prominently at the top of the report with a warning prefix. After the first week: transition from daily to weekly reporting (see step 15.8). Output: daily_health_report_sent.

steps.name: incident_response
steps.trigger: Triggered by monitoring alerts or detected outages
steps.instruction: If a production incident occurs, execute the incident response lifecycle. Detect: receive alert from monitoring (error spike, health check failure, user reports). Assess severity: S1 (service down, data at risk, all users affected), S2 (major feature broken, many users affected), S3 (minor feature degraded, some users affected), S4 (cosmetic issue, minimal impact). Mitigate: based on severity — restart services, rollback to last known good deployment, disable the offending feature via feature flag, scale up infrastructure if load-related. Communicate: update status page immediately with current status and ETA, send Telegram notification to human with severity and impact summary. Resolve: apply permanent fix (not just restart), deploy fix through hotfix process, verify fix resolves the issue, monitor for recurrence. Post-mortem: document timeline (detection time, response time, resolution time), root_cause (what broke and why), impact (users affected, duration, data implications), action_items (what to change to prevent recurrence — code fix, monitoring improvement, process change). Output: incident_id, severity, timeline, root_cause, resolution, action_items.

steps.name: weekly_analytics_review
steps.instruction: Review key performance indicators weekly. Acquisition: new_signups (total and by source: organic, referral, social, email), visit_to_signup_conversion_rate, landing_page_bounce_rate. Activation: onboarding_completion_rate, time_to_first_value (median time from signup to completing core action), activation_rate (users who completed core action / total signups). Retention: D1_retention, D7_retention, D30_retention (cohort analysis — are newer cohorts retaining better or worse?), DAU/WAU/MAU trends. Revenue (if applicable): MRR, new_subscriptions, churned_subscriptions, net_revenue_change, ARPU, trial_to_paid_conversion_rate. Referral: NPS score if measured, invite_rate (users who invited others / total active users). Compare all metrics against targets defined in success_metrics from Phase 2. Identify: trends (improving, stable, declining), anomalies (unexpected spikes or drops), correlations (e.g., users from source X retain better). Output: weekly_kpi_report, trends, anomalies, recommended_actions.

steps.name: weekly_summary
steps.instruction: Compress the weekly analytics review into a concise executive summary for the human. Structure: wins (what's going well — top 3 metrics trending positively or exceeding targets), concerns (what needs attention — metrics below target or trending negatively), decisions_needed (any strategic decisions requiring human input). Keep total summary under 300 words. If any KPI is significantly below target (more than 20% miss): present diagnosis (why is it underperforming? what data supports this?), present 2-3 options (e.g., increase marketing spend, adjust onboarding flow, revisit pricing), and trigger needs_clarification asking human for direction. Send summary via Telegram. Output: weekly_executive_summary, decisions_requested (if any).

steps.name: user_feedback_analysis
steps.instruction: Aggregate and analyze all user feedback from the past week across all channels (in-app feedback widget, support email, social media mentions, app store reviews, community forums). Categorize each piece: bug_report, feature_request, ux_complaint, praise, question, spam. For feature requests: track frequency (how many unique users requested the same thing), group similar requests into themes, map to existing backlog items if overlap exists. For UX complaints: identify the specific screen, flow, or interaction causing confusion — cross-reference with analytics to check if users who encounter that flow have lower retention. For bugs: deduplicate against known issues, assess severity. Aggregate: top_5_requested_features (with request count and representative quotes), top_5_pain_points (with frequency and affected flow), sentiment_trend (improving/stable/declining compared to last week), churn_correlation (are users who report specific complaints more likely to churn?). Output: weekly_feedback_report, actionable_insights.

steps.name: feature_request_prioritization
steps.instruction: Maintain and update a ranked feature request backlog. For each feature request (new or existing): score using ICE framework — Impact (1-10: how much value does this add to users? does it improve a key metric?), Confidence (1-10: how sure are we about the impact? based on: number of requests, analytics data supporting need, competitive analysis), Ease (1-10: how easy is it to implement? consider: engineering effort, design effort, dependencies, risk). Calculate ICE_score = Impact × Confidence × Ease. Rank all feature requests by ICE score. For each of the top 10: include request_count (how many users asked), representative_feedback (summarized user quotes), estimated_effort (T-shirt size: S/M/L/XL), strategic_alignment (does it reinforce or dilute the core value proposition and USP?). Flag any requests that conflict with the product vision. Output: ranked_feature_backlog, top_10_detailed, new_entries_this_week, removed_entries (if any were implemented or invalidated).

steps.name: bug_triage
steps.instruction: Triage all incoming bugs from the past week (from error monitoring, user feedback, QA). For each bug: assign severity — critical (data loss, security vulnerability, complete feature failure for all users), high (major feature broken for some users, payment issues), medium (degraded experience, workaround exists), low (cosmetic, minor inconvenience). Assign priority based on: severity × user_impact (how many users affected) × frequency (how often it occurs). Group bugs by component/feature area to identify systemic issues. For each bug: title, severity, priority, affected_component, reproduction_steps (if known), estimated_fix_effort, target_sprint (critical: immediate, high: this week, medium: this sprint, low: backlog). Track metrics: bugs_opened_this_week, bugs_closed_this_week, open_bug_count_by_severity, oldest_unresolved_bug. Output: triaged_bug_list, bug_metrics, systemic_issues_identified.

steps.name: bug_fixing
steps.instruction: Fix bugs based on triage priority. For each bug fix: 1) Reproduce the bug locally (write a failing test that demonstrates the bug), 2) Diagnose root cause (not just the symptom — understand why the bug exists), 3) Implement fix (minimal change targeting the root cause, avoid side effects), 4) Write regression test (the previously-failing test should now pass, plus any additional edge cases discovered during diagnosis), 5) Run full test suite (ensure fix doesn't break anything else), 6) Submit for code review, 7) Deploy (via normal deployment pipeline for high/medium/low, via hotfix process for critical). Track per fix: bug_id, root_cause, fix_description, tests_added, review_approved, deployed. Weekly metrics: bugs_fixed, average_fix_time, regression_tests_added. Output: fix_report, updated_bug_metrics.

steps.name: dependency_updates
steps.trigger: Monthly for routine updates, immediate for security advisories
steps.instruction: Perform dependency maintenance. Monthly routine: run dependency audit (npm audit / pip audit / equivalent for all package managers in use), list all outdated dependencies with current vs latest version, categorize updates as: patch (x.x.PATCH — safe, apply all), minor (x.MINOR.x — usually safe, review changelogs), major (MAJOR.x.x — breaking changes possible, evaluate carefully). Apply all patch updates, apply minor updates after reviewing changelogs for breaking changes, evaluate major updates (read migration guide, assess effort, schedule if beneficial). After all updates: run full test suite (unit + integration + E2E), verify no regressions, check bundle size hasn't increased significantly. For security advisories (from GitHub Dependabot, npm audit, or equivalent): critical/high severity — patch within 24 hours regardless of schedule, medium — patch within 1 week, low — include in next monthly cycle. Quarterly: evaluate major version upgrades for key dependencies (framework, ORM, UI library). Output: dependencies_updated, security_patches_applied, test_results_after_update, major_upgrades_evaluated.

steps.name: database_maintenance
steps.instruction: Perform monthly database maintenance. Slow query analysis: review slow query log (queries exceeding 100ms), identify top 10 slowest queries, for each: explain query plan, determine if missing index, N+1 query, or inefficient join — recommend and implement optimization. Index review: list all indexes, identify unused indexes (no reads in past 30 days — consider dropping to save write overhead and storage), identify missing indexes (queries doing sequential scans on large tables — add appropriate indexes). Table maintenance: check for table bloat (dead tuples in PostgreSQL — run VACUUM ANALYZE if needed), check disk usage growth rate (project when storage upgrade needed), verify auto-vacuum is running effectively. Backup verification: verify automated backups ran successfully every day this month (check backup logs), perform a test restore to a temporary instance (same as step 13.31 but routine), verify data integrity of restored backup, record restore time (must remain within RTO). Connection pool health: check max connections vs active connections, verify no connection leaks. Output: slow_queries_optimized, indexes_changed, table_health, backup_verified, restore_time.

steps.name: infrastructure_cost_review
steps.instruction: Review cloud infrastructure costs monthly. Pull billing data: total_cost, cost_by_service (compute, database, storage, bandwidth, CDN, monitoring, email, DNS, etc.), cost_trend (month-over-month change). Compare: actual_cost vs budget, cost_per_user (total cost / active users — is it sustainable at scale?). Identify optimization opportunities: underutilized resources (instances running below 20% CPU average — right-size to smaller tier), idle resources (development/staging resources that can be shut down off-hours), reserved instance opportunities (if usage is stable, reserved instances save 30-60%), storage optimization (old logs, unused backups, oversized volumes), bandwidth optimization (CDN coverage, image optimization reducing transfer costs). Calculate: current_monthly_cost, projected_annual_cost, cost_if_optimized, savings_potential. If cost significantly exceeds expectations (more than 20% over budget) or is trending unsustainably: trigger needs_clarification presenting cost breakdown, trend, optimization options with estimated savings, and ask human for budget decision. Output: cost_report, optimization_recommendations, projected_savings.

steps.name: performance_optimization
steps.trigger: Monthly, or triggered when performance degrades toward NFR thresholds
steps.instruction: Identify and resolve performance bottlenecks. Backend: identify slowest API endpoints from APM data (top 10 by p95 latency), for each: profile the request lifecycle (database time, external API time, compute time), optimize — add missing database indexes, implement or improve caching (Redis for frequently accessed data, HTTP cache headers for static content), fix N+1 queries (eager load associations), optimize serialization (reduce payload size, remove unnecessary fields). Frontend: identify slowest pages from frontend monitoring (Largest Contentful Paint, Total Blocking Time), optimize — lazy load below-the-fold components, optimize images (WebP, proper sizing, lazy loading), code split large bundles, minimize third-party script impact, implement proper caching headers. Database: optimize queries identified in database maintenance, add read replicas if read load is high. Measure: before and after for each optimization (latency, throughput, bundle size). Output: optimizations_applied, before_after_metrics, remaining_bottlenecks.

steps.name: technical_debt_tracking
steps.instruction: Maintain and review the technical debt register. For each debt item: title, description (what is the debt and how was it incurred — rushed implementation, known shortcut, outdated pattern, etc.), impact (how does it affect development: slows_development, causes_bugs, limits_scalability, reduces_maintainability — with specific examples), effort_to_fix (T-shirt size: S/M/L/XL with estimated hours), priority (based on impact × likelihood_of_causing_problems). Categories: code_debt (duplicated code, overly complex functions, missing abstractions), architecture_debt (monolith that should be split, wrong technology choice, missing queue/cache layer), test_debt (untested code paths, flaky tests, missing integration tests), infrastructure_debt (manual processes that should be automated, outdated configurations), documentation_debt (missing or outdated docs). Schedule 20% of each sprint's capacity for debt reduction (prevent debt from growing). Monthly review: add new items, update priorities, track items resolved, calculate debt_trend (growing/shrinking). Output: debt_register, items_resolved_this_month, new_items, debt_trend, next_sprint_debt_targets.

steps.name: security_patching
steps.trigger: Weekly monitoring, immediate for critical vulnerabilities
steps.instruction: Monitor and respond to security advisories. Weekly: check GitHub Dependabot alerts, npm/pip audit results, infrastructure provider security bulletins, CVE databases for dependencies in use. For each vulnerability: assess applicability (does it affect our usage of the dependency?), severity (critical, high, medium, low based on CVSS and our exposure), exploitability (is there a known exploit? is our surface exposed?). Patching SLA: critical — patch within 24 hours (immediate hotfix deployment), high — patch within 1 week, medium — patch within 1 month (next scheduled maintenance), low — next dependency update cycle. For each patch: apply update, run full test suite, verify the vulnerability is resolved (re-run scanner), deploy. Monthly: run comprehensive security scan against production (same as step 13.26 but routine) — check SSL configuration, security headers, open ports, dependency vulnerabilities, authentication endpoints. Output: vulnerabilities_monitored, patches_applied, scan_results, compliance_status.

steps.name: new_feature_specification
steps.instruction: For the next highest-priority feature from the ranked feature backlog: write a full specification following the same format used in Phase 2. Include: feature_name, user_story ('As a [user type], I want to [action] so that [benefit]'), motivation (reference the user feedback that drove this — how many users requested it, what pain point it addresses), acceptance_criteria (numbered list of specific, testable conditions that must be met for the feature to be considered complete), use_cases (primary: the main happy path, secondary: alternative flows, edge_cases: unusual but valid scenarios), scope (what's included in this iteration, what's explicitly excluded for later), dependencies (existing features or systems this interacts with), success_metrics (how will we measure if this feature is successful — specific KPI targets). Output: feature_specification.

steps.name: new_feature_design
steps.instruction: Design UI/UX for the new feature following the same format used in Phase 5. Create: screen_specifications (for each new or modified screen: layout, components, content, states — empty, loading, populated, error), component_specifications (for each new UI component: props, variants, states, responsive behavior, accessibility requirements), interaction_specifications (user flows, transitions, animations if any, loading states, success/error feedback), responsive_behavior (how the feature adapts across breakpoints: mobile, tablet, desktop), accessibility_requirements (WCAG compliance: keyboard navigation, screen reader support, color contrast, focus management). Extend the existing design system — reuse established components, patterns, colors, and typography. Only introduce new design patterns if the existing system genuinely cannot accommodate the feature. Document any new patterns added with rationale. Output: feature_design_spec.

steps.name: new_feature_design_review
steps.instruction: Review the new feature specification and design for quality and consistency. Check specification: acceptance criteria are testable and unambiguous, scope is clearly defined, use cases cover edge cases, success metrics are measurable. Check design: consistent with existing product UI (same patterns, components, spacing, typography), doesn't introduce unnecessary new patterns, responsive behavior is complete, accessibility requirements are specified, interaction flows are logical and efficient (minimal clicks to complete task). Check integration: the new feature doesn't conflict with or degrade existing features, navigation accommodates the new feature naturally, data model changes are backward compatible. If the feature represents a significant product direction change (new user type, new monetization model, major UX paradigm shift): trigger needs_clarification to confirm strategic alignment with human before proceeding to implementation. Output: review_approved (boolean), issues, strategic_concerns.

steps.name: new_feature_implementation
steps.instruction: Execute the feature implementation following the identical process established in Phase 8 (feature_implementation_template). This includes: create feature branch, implement backend (API endpoints, business logic, database migrations if needed, validation, error handling), implement frontend (components, state management, API integration, responsive layout, loading/error states), write unit tests (business logic, component rendering, edge cases), write integration tests (API endpoints, database interactions), write E2E tests (critical user flows for this feature), ensure code quality (linting, type checking, no console.logs), self-review (code review checklist from Phase 8). Submit for code review. Output: implementation_complete, branch_name, test_coverage, ready_for_review.

steps.name: release_planning
steps.instruction: Group completed features and bug fixes into a release. Assign version number following semantic versioning: major (breaking changes or significant new capability), minor (new features, backward compatible), patch (bug fixes only). For this release: list all included changes — new_features (with brief description and linked specification), bug_fixes (with bug ID, brief description, severity), improvements (performance optimizations, UX tweaks, dependency updates). Verify: all included changes have passed code review, all tests pass on develop branch, no unresolved merge conflicts, all linked issues/tickets are in 'ready for release' state. Plan release timeline: code freeze date, QA window (staging testing), release date. Write release notes draft (user-facing: what's new, what's fixed, what's improved). Output: release_plan, version_number, included_changes, release_notes_draft.

steps.name: release_notes
steps.instruction: Write polished release notes for two audiences. User-facing release notes: version number, release date, new_features (each with a brief compelling description focused on user benefit, not technical implementation), improvements (noticeable UX or performance enhancements), bug_fixes (only mention fixes users would have noticed — skip internal-only fixes), tone: enthusiastic but concise, focus on value and benefits. Developer-facing CHANGELOG: version number, date, categorized list (Added, Changed, Fixed, Deprecated, Removed, Security) following Keep a Changelog format, include technical details, reference issue/ticket numbers. If the release includes a significant new feature: write a brief announcement blog post draft (problem → solution → how to use it → what's next). Output: user_release_notes, changelog_entry, blog_post_draft (if applicable).

steps.name: release_deployment
steps.instruction: Execute the release deployment. Steps: 1) Merge develop branch into main (or release branch into main per branching strategy), 2) Tag the release with version number (git tag vX.Y.Z), 3) Trigger production deployment pipeline (CI/CD should handle: build, test, deploy), 4) Monitor deployment progress (watch CI/CD pipeline logs, verify each stage passes), 5) Verify deployment completed (health endpoint returns new version number), 6) Run post-deployment smoke tests (same as step 14.5 smoke test: landing page, signup, core flow, billing), 7) Monitor error tracking for 30 minutes (watch for new error types introduced by this release), 8) Verify no performance regressions (compare p95 latency before and after deployment). If any step fails or regression detected: execute rollback (deploy previous version tag), diagnose issue, fix, and re-attempt. Output: deployment_successful, version_deployed, smoke_test_results, monitoring_clear.

steps.name: release_announcement
steps.instruction: Announce the new release to users through appropriate channels. In-app notification: for active users, display a non-intrusive notification (banner or toast) highlighting the key new feature or improvement with a dismiss option and a 'Learn more' link. Email: for users opted-in to product updates, send a brief email: subject line mentioning the key new feature, body with 2-3 highlights and CTA to try the new feature, unsubscribe link. Blog post: if a significant feature was released, publish the blog post draft from release_notes step. Social media: brief update on Twitter/X and LinkedIn announcing the new feature or improvements — link to blog post or changelog. App store: if mobile app was updated, update the 'What's New' section in App Store and Play Store listing. Output: announcement_channels_used, notifications_sent.

steps.name: post_release_monitoring
steps.instruction: Heightened monitoring for 24 hours after each release. Monitor: new_error_types (errors that didn't exist before this release — likely caused by new code), error_rate_change (compare to pre-release baseline — any increase >0.1% is a concern), performance_regressions (p95 latency increase >10% on any endpoint — check if new feature endpoints are slow), user_complaints (monitor feedback channels for reports related to new features), feature_specific_metrics (is the new feature being used? are users completing the expected flows? any unexpected drop-offs?). Every 4 hours: compile monitoring summary. If critical issues detected (new errors affecting >1% of users, performance regression >25%, data integrity concerns): initiate rollback immediately and alert human. If minor issues detected: create bug reports, prioritize in next sprint. After 24 hours with no critical issues: downgrade to normal monitoring cadence. Output: post_release_monitoring_log, issues_detected, rollback_needed (boolean).

steps.name: iteration_retrospective
steps.instruction: After each release cycle is complete (feature shipped, monitored, stable): conduct an iteration retrospective. Analyze: what_went_well (efficient steps, good decisions, smooth processes — be specific), what_didnt_go_well (bottlenecks, delays, bugs that slipped through, unclear requirements — be specific), process_improvements (concrete changes to make the next iteration better). Metrics: cycle_time (days from specification to production — is it improving?), defect_rate (bugs found after deployment / total changes — is it declining?), velocity_trend (are we delivering more or less per iteration?), quality_trend (test coverage change, bug count trend, user-reported issues trend). Compare to previous iterations: identify patterns (recurring problems suggest systemic issues). Propose specific process changes (e.g., add a design review step, improve test coverage threshold, change code review checklist). Output: retrospective_report, process_changes_proposed, updated_velocity_metrics.

steps.name: roadmap_update
steps.instruction: Quarterly review and update of the product roadmap. Inputs: user_feedback_trends (from weekly feedback analyses — what are users consistently asking for?), analytics_data (from weekly analytics — which metrics need improvement? where are users dropping off?), competitive_landscape (search for competitor updates, new entrants, market shifts), technology_changes (new tools, frameworks, or APIs that could improve the product or reduce costs), business_performance (revenue trend, growth rate, churn rate, unit economics). Re-evaluate: current roadmap items (still relevant? priority changed?), feature backlog (top items still aligned with strategy?), technical debt register (any debt items now blocking growth?). Propose: next_quarter_priorities (top 3-5 initiatives with rationale), items_to_deprioritize (what to remove or push back, with rationale), strategic_pivots (if data suggests a direction change — e.g., focus on retention over acquisition, pivot to a different user segment, add or remove a pricing tier). Trigger needs_clarification presenting the roadmap proposal with supporting data, asking human for strategic direction decisions and final approval. Output: proposed_roadmap, supporting_data, human_approved_roadmap.
